Alternatively, extend one arm and pull fingers back with other hand.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Wrist flexor stretch — counteracts grip-intensive exercise and computer work.
whyForYou: Prevents forearm tightness after deadlifts, carries, and daily computer use.
proTip: Do this every hour at your desk — prevents cumulative forearm tension.

name: Neck Side Bend Stretch
steps: Sit or stand tall. Look straight ahead.
Tilt right ear toward right shoulder.
Place right hand gently on left side of head.
Very light pressure — 4/10 stretch. Hold 30s. Switch.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hold opposite shoulder down — grip chair if needed.

purpose: SCM and scalene stretch — releases lateral neck tension from desk work and stress.
whyForYou: Desk work and stress create lateral neck tension. This releases it safely.
proTip: Look slightly UP for SCM emphasis, slightly DOWN for scalene emphasis.

name: Standing Calf Stretch (Wall)
steps: Face wall. Step one foot back. Back heel stays DOWN.
Lean into wall — feel stretch in back calf. Leg STRAIGHT. 30s.
Then BEND back knee — targets soleus. 15s.
Switch legs.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Lean from ankle, not rounding back.
injuryNotes.knee: Bent-knee phase gentle on left knee. Stop if discomfort.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Just leaning on wall.

purpose: Calf flexibility — tight calves restrict ankle dorsiflexion, affecting squats and gait.
whyForYou: Tight calves restrict your ankle mobility, which chains up to your knee and back.
proTip: Do BOTH phases — straight AND bent knee. They hit different muscles.

name: Diaphragmatic Breathing (90/90)
steps: Lie on back. Feet on wall — hips and knees at 90°.
Place one hand on chest, one on belly.
Inhale through NOSE — belly hand rises, chest hand stays STILL.
Exhale through mouth — belly falls. 4 in, 6 out.
coreBracing: NONE — this IS core activation in its most fundamental form.

injuryNotes.lower_back: The foundation of your core rehab. Diaphragm is the roof of your core canister. Train it first.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Parasympathetic activation + diaphragm training. The foundation of all core work.
whyForYou: The diaphragm is the roof of your core canister. Before you can brace, you must breathe properly. Start every session here.
proTip: 90/90 position (feet on wall) puts your pelvis in optimal position for diaphragmatic access.
source: PRI (Postural Restoration Institute), DNS (Dynamic Neuromuscular Stabilization)

name: Box Breathing (4-4-4-4)
steps: Sit or lie comfortably. Close eyes.
Inhale through nose — 4 counts.
HOLD — 4 counts.
Exhale through mouth — 4 counts. HOLD empty — 4 counts.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact. Calming for pain management.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Stress regulation and nervous system calming — used by Navy SEALs and first responders.
whyForYou: Pre-workout calming. Also useful for pain management and stress reduction in daily life.
proTip: Do 5 rounds before stressful situations (work meetings, difficult conversations). Takes 2 minutes.
source: Stanford research on controlled breathing, Military performance literature

name: Crocodile Breathing (Prone)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Belly pushes into floor with each breath

steps: Lie face down. Forehead on stacked hands.
Breathe into your BELLY — feel it push into the floor.
Your back should rise and fall slightly with each breath.
4 count inhale, 6 count exhale. 5 minutes.
formCues: ✅ Belly pushes INTO floor on inhale

breathing.pattern: Belly into floor on inhale, floor provides feedback

injuryNotes.lower_back: Excellent position — floor prevents chest breathing and forces diaphragmatic pattern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Forehead on hands. Adjust if shoulder is uncomfortable.

purpose: Teaches diaphragmatic breathing using floor as biofeedback — can't cheat when belly hits the floor.
whyForYou: If you can't feel the diaphragm working in 90/90, try this. The floor gives you feedback.
proTip: The floor doesn't lie — if your belly doesn't push into it, you're chest breathing.

name: Physiological Sigh (Double Inhale + Long Exhale)
steps: Take a normal inhale through your nose.
IMMEDIATELY take a second short 'sip' of air on top — fills the remaining lung capacity.
Long, slow exhale through the mouth — let it ALL out.
That's one physiological sigh. Repeat 3-5 times.
formCues: ✅ TWO inhales — one big, one small 'sip'
✅ The double inhale is the key
commonMistakes: ❌ Only one inhale (missing the second sip)

breathing.pattern: Double nasal inhale (big + sip), long mouth exhale

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact. Excellent for pain-related anxiety.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Fastest real-time stress reduction technique — reinflates collapsed alveoli and activates vagus nerve.
whyForYou: Your fastest tool for acute stress, anxiety, or pain-related tension. Works in 1-3 breaths.
proTip: The double inhale reinflates collapsed alveoli in your lungs — that's why it works so fast. Stanford proved it.
source: Huberman Lab, Stanford University (Balban et al. 2023, Cell Reports Medicine)

name: Seated Chest Press (Band)

prerequisites.notes: No prerequisites — seated pushing accessible to all

steps: Sit in sturdy chair. Band anchored behind you at chest height.
Hold band handles at chest level. Elbows at 45°.
Press forward until arms are extended.
Return slowly. Control the band.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Shoulder severity ≤3. Band provides variable resistance — easier at start, harder at end.

purpose: Seated horizontal push — accessible pressing for wheelchair users, elderly, or anyone who can't stand.
whyForYou: Seated alternative when you can't do push-ups or standing pressing due to back or knee issues.
proTip: Anchor band around the back of the chair for the cleanest line of resistance.

name: Seated Shoulder Press (DB/Band)

prerequisites.notes: Phase 2+. Shoulder severity ≤2. Landmine press mastered.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps with DBs, no shoulder pain

steps: Sit with back support. DBs at shoulder height, neutral or pronated grip.
Press straight UP. Don't lock out aggressively.
Lower to shoulder height. Don't go below shoulders.
Keep back against pad.
formCues: ✅ Don't go below shoulder height on descent

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated with back support — minimal spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED currently (severity 2 = gate). Overhead movement requires labrum improvement. Never behind neck. No end-range.

purpose: Seated overhead pressing — for shoulder development with back support.
whyForYou: Future overhead pressing option once your labrum improves. Seated reduces spinal load.
proTip: Neutral grip (palms facing each other) is always safest for your labrum.

name: Seated Lateral Raise (DB, Light)
steps: Sit with back support. Light DBs at sides.
Raise arms to sides — stop at shoulder height (90°).
Hold 1s. Lower slowly.
Light weight — this is isolation.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated with support.
injuryNotes.shoulder: STOP at 90°. Shoulder severity ≤2. Light weight only. Same rules as standing lateral raise.

purpose: Seated lateral raise — for shoulder aesthetics with back support.
whyForYou: Seated version eliminates momentum and back arch. Safer for both your back and shoulder.
proTip: Seated eliminates cheating — you'll use less weight but get more medial delt activation.

name: Seated Row (Band)
steps: Sit on floor, legs extended. Band around feet.
Hold band ends. Sit tall.
Pull elbows BACK — squeeze shoulder blades. Hold 1s.
Return slowly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated on floor — minimal spinal load. Stay upright.
injuryNotes.knee: Legs extended — no knee flexion stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Neutral grip. No overhead.

purpose: Seated pulling — accessible horizontal pull for all levels and abilities.
whyForYou: When cable row isn't available, this band version maintains your pulling pattern anywhere.
proTip: Sit on a folded towel to elevate hips if hamstrings are tight and you can't sit upright.

name: Seated Bicep Curl
steps: Sit with back support. DBs at sides, palms forward.
Curl both up. Elbows pinned.
Squeeze at top. Lower 2s.
Full extension at bottom.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated with support — eliminates back swing.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hammer curl (neutral) if supinated bothers labrum.

purpose: Seated bicep curl — eliminates back swing for stricter isolation.
whyForYou: Back support eliminates cheating — stricter bicep isolation.
proTip: Seated forces honest reps. You'll use less weight but build more actual bicep.

name: Seated Tricep Extension (DB/Band)
steps: Sit with back support. Hold one DB with both hands behind head.
Elbows point UP. Extend DB overhead.
Lower behind head slowly — elbows stay pointed up.
Don't flare elbows.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated with support.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Overhead position. BLOCKED for you currently (severity 2 = gate). Use tricep pushdown instead.

purpose: Seated overhead tricep extension — targets long head of triceps.
whyForYou: Overhead tricep option once shoulder tolerates. Until then, use cable pushdown.
proTip: Band attached behind and above you works just as well if no DB available.

name: Seated Band Pull-Apart
steps: Sit tall. Band at shoulder height, arms straight.
Pull apart — squeeze blades. Hold 1s.
Return slowly.
Light band — high reps.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Same as standing version. Excellent for labrum.

purpose: Seated rear delt and scapular retraction — wheelchair-accessible shoulder health exercise.
whyForYou: Same shoulder benefit as standing version. Can be done in any chair.
proTip: Do 20 of these between every set of pressing — even at your desk.

name: Seated Trunk Rotation
steps: Sit tall in chair. Arms crossed on chest.
Rotate torso to RIGHT — only as far as comfortable.
Hold 1s. Rotate to LEFT.
Keep hips facing forward — only torso rotates.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Rotation from THORACIC spine only. Back severity ≤3. If any back pain, use bird dog instead.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms crossed. No shoulder stress.

purpose: Seated thoracic rotation — mobility and light core work from a chair.
whyForYou: Seated rotation that's accessible at your desk. Keeps thoracic rotation alive throughout the day.
proTip: Hold a light medicine ball for added resistance once bodyweight is easy.

name: Seated Punches (Shadow Boxing)
steps: Sit tall. Fists up at chin level.
Throw alternating punches — jab, cross, hook.
Keep core engaged. Rotate torso with each punch.
30s on, 30s rest. 3 rounds.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — low load. Rotation is from thoracic.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Don't hyperextend punches. Shoulder severity ≤3. Modify range if needed.

purpose: Seated cardiovascular training — raises heart rate without standing or impact.
whyForYou: Cardio you can do seated. Great for days when back or knee won't allow walking.
proTip: Add light 1-2 lb dumbbells for more shoulder endurance work — but only if shoulder tolerates.

name: Chair Dips

prerequisites.notes: Shoulder severity ≤1. Dips stress the anterior shoulder significantly.

steps: Hands on chair edge behind you. Feet extended forward.
Lower body by bending elbows — 90° max depth.
Push back up — lock out.
Keep elbows pointing BACK, not out.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No direct concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED for you (severity 2 > gate 1). Dips put significant stress on anterior shoulder. Only when labrum severity is 1. Future exercise.

purpose: Bodyweight tricep and shoulder pressing using a chair — no equipment needed.
whyForYou: Future exercise once labrum is fully recovered. Dips are too stressful for your current shoulder.
proTip: Bend knees to reduce load. Straighten legs to increase load. Elevate feet for maximum difficulty.

name: Seated Crunch
steps: Sit at edge of chair. Hands crossed on chest.
Curl torso forward toward thighs. Exhale hard.
Hold 1s at bottom. Return to upright.
Small controlled movement — not a big crunch.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Spinal flexion — back severity ≤3. If disc-related, use dead bug instead. This is for non-disc conditions.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Seated core flexion — accessible core work for wheelchair users or those who can't get on the floor.
whyForYou: Chair alternative to floor core work. Use when floor exercises aren't accessible.
proTip: The exhale IS the exercise — blow out all the air hard. The abs contract reflexively.

name: Seated Oblique Twist
steps: Sit at edge of chair. Hands at temples or holding light weight.
Rotate and crunch — right elbow toward left knee direction.
Return to center. Rotate other direction.
Controlled — not fast twisting.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Rotation + flexion — back severity ≤3. Rotate from THORACIC only. Contraindicated for disc patients.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Seated oblique work — rotation + flexion pattern from a chair.
whyForYou: Seated rotational core work. Only when back tolerates rotation.
proTip: Hold a medicine ball or weight for added resistance. Keep it controlled.

name: Seated Leg Extension (Bodyweight/Band)

prerequisites.notes: Seated quad work — accessible to all

steps: Sit in chair. One foot on floor.
Extend other leg straight out. Squeeze quad at top.
Hold 1s. Lower slowly.
Complete all reps, switch legs.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: Open chain — some knee conditions prefer closed chain. If painful, switch to quad sets. Knee severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Seated quad isolation — accessible strengthening without standing.
whyForYou: Chair-based quad work for days when you can't stand or get on the floor.
proTip: Add a band around the chair leg and your ankle for resistance.

name: Seated Marching

prerequisites.notes: No prerequisites — most basic seated cardio

steps: Sit tall in chair. Feet flat on floor.
Alternately lift knees — march in place.
Pump arms if able. Keep pace steady.
30s on, 30s rest.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Hip flexion only — low knee stress. If left knee is very sensitive, do ankle pumps instead.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Pump arms if shoulder tolerates.

purpose: Most basic seated cardio — raises heart rate with zero impact.
whyForYou: Cardio option for days when nothing else is tolerated. Gets blood moving.
proTip: Hold light DBs in hands while marching for more upper body engagement.

name: Seated Heel Slides
steps: Sit in chair. One foot on smooth surface (towel helps).
Slide heel forward — extending knee.
Slide back — bending knee. Go to comfortable range.
Slow and gentle. Track ROM progress.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Gentle ROM exercise. Safe even immediately post-op. Go to comfortable range only.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Seated knee ROM exercise — gentle flexion/extension for knee rehab.
whyForYou: Maintains and improves your left knee ROM in a gentle, controlled way.
proTip: Use a towel under the foot on a hard floor — reduces friction for easier sliding.

name: Seated Ankle Pumps

prerequisites.notes: Most basic exercise — DVT prevention + ankle ROM

steps: Sit with legs extended or feet hanging.
Point toes DOWN (plantarflex), then pull toes UP (dorsiflex).
Alternate pumping — 20-30 reps.
Do hourly if bed-bound or sitting for extended periods.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: DVT prevention + ankle ROM — the most basic exercise in existence. Done hourly when sedentary.
whyForYou: DVT prevention during long sitting, post-surgical recovery, or travel. Also maintains ankle ROM.
proTip: Set a phone timer every hour — do 20 pumps. Critical for long flights and desk work.

name: Seated Hip Abduction (Band)
steps: Sit in chair. Band around thighs above knees.
Push knees APART against band. Hold 1s.
Return slowly — don't let band snap back.
Keep feet on floor throughout.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Band above knees. Avoid if knee is aggravated. Knee severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Seated glute med activation — wheelchair-accessible hip stabilizer strengthening.
whyForYou: Glute med work from a chair. Protects your knee even when you can't stand.
proTip: Do sets of these at your desk throughout the day — accumulated volume builds glute med endurance.

name: Captain's Chair Lift

prerequisites.notes: Phase 2+. Requires upper body strength and core stability.

steps: Sit in chair with armrests. Hands on armrests.
Push DOWN — lift your body off the chair.
Hold 1-2s. Lower slowly.
Requires significant upper body strength.
commonMistakes: ❌ Not enough arm strength (work up to it)

injuryNotes.lower_back: Significant core demand. Severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact — not weight-bearing through legs.
injuryNotes.shoulder: High shoulder load. Severity ≤2. Major demand on shoulder extensors.

purpose: Full body lift from armrests — builds upper body pressing strength and core engagement.
whyForYou: Advanced seated exercise. Tests your upper body pushing strength in a functional way.
proTip: Start by just pushing and partially lifting. Full lift-off is the progression.

name: Ankle Pumps (Supine)

prerequisites.notes: Most basic exercise — safe for anyone

steps: Lie in bed. Legs straight.
Point toes DOWN, then pull toes UP.
Pump rhythmically — 20-30 reps.
Do every 1-2 hours when bed-bound.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine — no concern. Can be done in bed.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: DVT prevention — the most basic exercise for immobilized or bed-bound individuals.
whyForYou: Essential if you're ever bed-bound from a back flare. Prevents blood clots in the legs.
proTip: Set a timer every 1-2 hours. These save lives for post-surgical and immobilized patients.
source: DVT prevention protocols, Post-surgical care guidelines

name: Heel Slides (Supine)

prerequisites.notes: Basic knee ROM — safe for all

steps: Lie on back. One leg straight.
Slide heel along bed/floor — bend knee as far as comfortable.
Slide back out to straight. Repeat.
Track range of motion progress weekly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine — back supported.
injuryNotes.knee: Fundamental knee ROM exercise. Safe immediately post-op. Go to comfort only.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Supine knee ROM exercise — most basic knee flexion/extension for bed-bound individuals.
whyForYou: If you're ever bed-bound from a flare, this maintains your knee ROM while lying down.
proTip: Plastic bag under heel on sheets reduces friction for easier sliding.

name: Supine Pelvic Tilt

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps with back flat to floor

steps: Lie on back. Knees bent, feet flat.
Flatten lower back INTO bed/floor — eliminate the arch.
Hold 5s. Feel abs engage.
Release. Repeat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Safest core exercise — can be done even during acute flare. Foundation of core rehab.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Most basic core activation — foundation for all core work. Safe even in acute pain.
whyForYou: When your back is flaring and you can't do anything else, you can do this. Foundation of your core rehab.
proTip: This is where EVERYONE starts their core rehab. Master this before dead bug.

name: Supine Hip Abduction/Adduction
steps: Lie on back. Legs straight.
Slide one leg OUT to the side (abduction). Keep toe up.
Slide back to center (adduction).
Repeat. Then switch legs.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine — back supported.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Supine hip ROM — maintains hip abduction/adduction while lying down.
whyForYou: Maintains hip mobility during bed rest. Keeps hip ROM alive when you can't stand.
proTip: Plastic bag under heel for less friction. Do while watching TV.

name: Supine Shoulder Flexion (Arm Raise)
steps: Lie on back. Arms at sides.
Raise one arm overhead toward the floor behind you.
Go to comfortable range. Hold 1s.
Return slowly. Switch arms.
formCues: ✅ Use other arm to assist if needed

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Gravity-eliminated makes this easier than standing. If even this hurts, use pendulum swing. Shoulder severity ≤4.

purpose: Supine shoulder flexion ROM — gravity-eliminated position makes overhead movement easier.
whyForYou: Supine overhead movement is easier than standing — gravity helps rather than resists. Good for maintaining left shoulder ROM.
proTip: Use the unaffected arm to assist (AAROM) — grab the wrist and help it overhead.

name: Diaphragmatic Breathing (Supine)
steps: Lie on back. Knees bent optional.
Hand on belly, hand on chest.
Breathe into BELLY — belly hand rises, chest stays still.
4 count in, 6 count out. 5-10 minutes.

injuryNotes.lower_back: The foundation exercise even during acute pain. Can be done in bed.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Bed-bound diaphragmatic breathing — core and nervous system regulation from bed.
whyForYou: When everything hurts and you can't move — this is where you start. Calms pain, activates core.
proTip: This is the FIRST exercise after any surgery or acute flare. Before anything else.

name: Gentle Neck Rotation (Supine)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full rotation both directions without pain

steps: Lie on back. No pillow (or flat pillow).
Slowly rotate head RIGHT — look toward right shoulder. Hold 2s.
Return to center. Rotate LEFT. Hold 2s.
Gentle, pain-free range only.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Gentlest cervical rotation — gravity-eliminated position. For acute neck pain or bed-bound individuals.
whyForYou: If your neck is acutely painful, this is the starting point before McKenzie neck exercises.
proTip: Supine position eliminates gravity — making this the easiest possible neck rotation.

name: Supine Glute Set
steps: Lie on back. Legs straight or knees bent.
SQUEEZE glutes as hard as possible. Hold 5s.
Release. Rest 2s. Squeeze again.
You should see/feel the glutes contract.
commonMistakes: ❌ Lifting hips (that's a bridge — this is just squeezing)

injuryNotes.lower_back: No hip movement — just squeezing. Safe even in acute back pain.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Most basic glute activation — isometric squeeze without any hip movement.
whyForYou: When a bridge is too much for your back, this activates the glutes without any movement.
proTip: Place hand under one glute — if you can't feel it contract, the mind-muscle connection needs work.

name: Water Walking

prerequisites.notes: Pool access required. Safe for all conditions.

steps: Enter pool — chest-depth water ideal.
Walk forward, backward, and sideways.
Exaggerate arm swings for resistance.
Start 15 min, build to 30 min.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Buoyancy reduces spinal load 50-90%. Excellent for acute back pain.
injuryNotes.knee: Water reduces joint load dramatically. Best option for acute knee pain.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arm swings optional — skip if shoulder is aggravated.

purpose: Aquatic cardio — buoyancy reduces joint load 50-90% while maintaining cardiovascular benefit.
whyForYou: The safest cardio option for ALL your injuries simultaneously. Water protects every joint.
proTip: Chest-depth water gives ~50% body weight reduction. Neck-depth gives ~90%.

name: Pool Wall Push-Up
steps: Stand in pool facing wall. Hands on wall at shoulder height.
Lean in — lower chest toward wall.
Push back. Buoyancy assists.
Adjust distance from wall for difficulty.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Water supports body. Minimal spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Buoyancy reduces load significantly. Good starting point for shoulder rehab pressing.

purpose: Aquatic push-up — water buoyancy reduces the load for joint-friendly pressing.
whyForYou: Lightest possible pressing load. Tests your shoulder tolerance before progressing to land-based push-ups.
proTip: Water provides both assistance (buoyancy) and resistance — unique training stimulus.

name: Aqua Jogging (Deep Water)

prerequisites.notes: Swimming ability required. Water walking mastered.

steps: Deep water — feet don't touch bottom. Flotation belt recommended.
Mimic running form — high knees, pumping arms.
Stay vertical — don't lean forward.
Start 15 min, build to 30+.

breathing.pattern: Continuous breathing — higher intensity than walking

injuryNotes.lower_back: ZERO impact. Excellent back-safe cardio. Deep water = zero spinal compression.
injuryNotes.knee: ZERO impact. Best cardio for acute knee problems.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arm pumping is optional if shoulder is bothered.

purpose: Zero-impact running — maintains cardiovascular fitness with absolutely zero joint impact.
whyForYou: Running fitness without any joint impact. Best of both worlds for your injuries.
proTip: A flotation belt makes this sustainable for 30+ minutes. Without it, your core fatigues first.
source: Aquatic therapy protocols, Running injury rehab

name: Pool Flutter Kicks
steps: Hold pool wall. Body horizontal, face in/out of water.
Flutter kick — alternating small, fast kicks.
Keep core engaged — don't let hips sag.
30s on, 30s rest. 3 rounds.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Water supports body. If back sags, reduce intensity. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: Straight legs — no significant knee flexion stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Holding wall — minimal demand.

purpose: Aquatic core + hip flexor work — water provides resistance while supporting the body.
whyForYou: Core work in water — water supports your body while your core works to stay straight.
proTip: Use a kick board for more core challenge — no wall support.

name: Water Resistance Arm Circles

prerequisites.notes: Water provides gentle resistance — safe for all

steps: Stand in chest-depth water. Arms at sides.
Make small circles with arms — water provides gentle resistance.
Gradually make circles larger as tolerated.
10 forward, 10 backward. Each arm.
formCues: ✅ Start small — increase size as tolerated

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern — standing in water.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Water provides GENTLE resistance — much safer than land-based ROM. Excellent for early shoulder rehab with labrum.

purpose: Shoulder ROM with water resistance — gentle, progressive, and joint-friendly.
whyForYou: The gentlest way to work shoulder ROM under resistance. Water provides support AND resistance simultaneously.
proTip: Slower = more resistance. Faster = more cardio. Adjust pace to your goal.

name: Pool Squat

prerequisites.notes: Pool required. Buoyancy allows deeper squat than land.

steps: Stand in chest-depth water. Feet shoulder-width.
Squat down — buoyancy reduces load dramatically.
Deeper squat may be possible vs land (less load).
Drive through heels to stand.
commonMistakes: ❌ Not squatting deep enough (water allows more)

breathing.pattern: Inhale at top, exhale during squat

injuryNotes.lower_back: Buoyancy dramatically reduces spinal load. May be able to squat deeper than on land.
injuryNotes.knee: Water reduces knee load significantly. May allow deeper squat than land for your left knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Buoyancy-assisted squat — allows deeper, pain-free squatting than possible on land.
whyForYou: Water lets you squat deeper with less load on your knee and back. Builds the pattern for land-based squats.
proTip: Pool squats let you practice full depth that you might not tolerate on land. The pattern transfers.

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat

prerequisites.notes: BW squat mastered. Same as DB goblet squat but KB grip.

steps: Hold KB by horns at chest. Feet shoulder-width.
Squat — elbows between knees at bottom.
Drive through heels. Stay upright.
Same form as dumbbell goblet squat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Front load reduces spinal shear. Stay upright.
injuryNotes.knee: Same depth rules as DB goblet squat. VMO pre-activation. Knee severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: KB handle may be easier or harder than DB for left shoulder — test both.

purpose: Goblet squat with kettlebell — same benefits as DB version with different grip stimulus.
whyForYou: Same great squat pattern as DB goblet squat. KB handle provides a different grip option.
proTip: KB goblet position is slightly more comfortable for many people than holding a dumbbell.

name: Kettlebell Deadlift
steps: KB on floor between feet. Hip-width stance.
Hinge at hips — grab handle with both hands. Back FLAT.
Drive through heels. Squeeze glutes at top.
Lower to floor with hip hinge — back stays FLAT.
formCues: ✅ KB between feet, not in front

breathing.pattern: Inhale on descent, brace, exhale at top

injuryNotes.lower_back: Hinge pattern — back must stay FLAT. Severity ≤3. Start very light. Same rules as DB RDL.
injuryNotes.knee: Slight knee bend. Not a deep squat pattern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Just holding KB — no stress.

purpose: Kettlebell hinge — teaches the deadlift pattern with a centrally loaded implement.
whyForYou: KB between the feet naturally teaches good hip hinge mechanics. Bridge between glute bridge and DB RDL.
proTip: The KB handle is above the ground — shorter range than a barbell deadlift. Good for learning.

name: Kettlebell Swing (Russian)

prerequisites.notes: Phase 2+. Hinge mastered. Explosive movement — requires solid foundation.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: RDL and KB deadlift mastered, Phase 2+, back severity ≤2

steps: Hike KB between legs — hinge back. Arms straight.
DRIVE hips forward explosively. Squeeze glutes HARD.
KB swings to chest/shoulder height (Russian — not overhead).
Let gravity pull KB back. Hinge. Repeat.
formCues: ✅ HIPS drive the swing — not arms
commonMistakes: ❌ Using arms to lift (should be hip power)

breathing.pattern: Sharp exhale at top of each swing

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED currently (severity 3 > gate 2). Explosive hip hinge with ballistic component. Requires excellent back health. Future exercise.
injuryNotes.knee: Low knee flexion — mostly hip. Knee severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: KB goes to chest height — moderate shoulder demand. Severity ≤3.

purpose: Explosive hip hinge — builds power, cardiovascular fitness, and posterior chain strength simultaneously.
whyForYou: Future exercise once back severity drops to ≤2. Builds the explosive hip power you need for surfing and BJJ.
proTip: The swing is a HINGE not a SQUAT. If your quads burn, you're squatting. Glutes should burn.

name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 5 reps each side with shoe on fist

steps: Lie on back, KB in right hand pressed to ceiling. Right knee bent.
Roll to left elbow, then left hand. Bridge hips.
Sweep left leg to half-kneeling. Stand.
Reverse EVERY step. KB stays locked overhead.

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED (severity 3 > gate 2). Every spinal position loaded.
injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED (severity 2 = gate 2, borderline). Half-kneeling and standing under overhead load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED (severity 2 = gate 2, borderline). Full overhead lockout throughout.

purpose: Ultimate full-body integration — every position in one exercise.
whyForYou: Mastery goal. Currently blocked by all three injuries. Represents complete athletic recovery.
proTip: Practice with a shoe balanced on your fist first. If the shoe falls, form broke.

name: Kettlebell Single-Arm Press
steps: Clean KB to rack position (at shoulder). Stand tall.
Press overhead — keep wrist straight, KB rests on forearm.
Lower to rack. Repeat. Then switch arms.
Anti-rotation demand is significant — brace hard.
formCues: ✅ Wrist straight — KB rests on forearm

injuryNotes.lower_back: Asymmetric overhead loading. Back severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED currently (severity 2 = gate). Overhead pressing. ER warm-up mandatory.

purpose: Unilateral overhead press — KB forces anti-rotation while building pressing strength.
whyForYou: Future pressing progression once your labrum improves. The anti-rotation component builds core simultaneously.
proTip: Think of pulling your body UNDER the KB rather than pressing it up.

name: Kettlebell Row (Single Arm)
steps: Staggered stance. Non-working hand on bench.
KB in working hand. Back FLAT.
Pull KB to hip. Squeeze shoulder blade. Hold 1s.
Lower slowly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Same rules as SA DB row. Back FLAT. Severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Neutral grip. Don't force stretch at bottom.

purpose: Single-arm rowing with kettlebell — offset handle adds grip challenge.
whyForYou: Same as SA DB row but KB handle adds grip work. Home-friendly pulling.
proTip: KB offset handle forces more grip engagement than a dumbbell.

name: Kettlebell Halo
steps: Hold KB by horns (bottom up) at chest.
Circle KB around your head — passing behind.
8 circles clockwise, 8 counterclockwise.
Keep KB LIGHT — this is mobility, not strength.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Light — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Circles behind head stress shoulder. Shoulder severity ≤3. Start with small circles and increase. Stop if pinching.

purpose: Shoulder mobility under light load — circles the shoulder through its full ROM.
whyForYou: Builds shoulder mobility and stability simultaneously. Light enough for warm-up.
proTip: Bottom-up KB grip is harder and builds more stability. Start with horn grip.

name: Kettlebell Farmer Carry
steps: Pick up KBs — one each hand. Hinge to lift.
Stand TALL. Shoulders back and down.
Walk 40s. Short, controlled steps.
Set down with hinge.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Same as DB farmer carry. Hinge to pick up/set down. Severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Grip may challenge shoulder — reduce weight if needed.

purpose: KB farmer carry — KB handle provides different grip stimulus than dumbbells.
whyForYou: KB handles are thicker — more grip challenge. Builds forearm and core endurance.
proTip: KB handles are thicker than DBs — expect to use slightly less weight.

name: TRX Row
steps: Grab TRX handles. Lean back — body straight, heels on floor.
PULL chest to hands. Squeeze blades.
Lower controlled. Body stays rigid.
Adjust angle for difficulty — more upright = easier.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Body must stay rigid. Severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Adjust angle — more upright = less shoulder demand. Neutral grip available.

purpose: Suspension row — adjustable difficulty by changing body angle. Great for home training.
whyForYou: Home-friendly pulling with adjustable difficulty. Door-mount TRX = gym-quality rows at home.
proTip: Feet further forward = harder. Feet under anchor = easier. Infinite adjustment.

name: TRX Push-Up

prerequisites.notes: Floor push-up mastered. Core stability required for instability.

steps: Hands in TRX handles. Plank position — feet on floor.
Lower chest between handles. 2s descent.
Push up. Instability forces massive core engagement.
Body stays rigid.
formCues: ✅ MUCH harder than floor push-up — instability
commonMistakes: ❌ Going to failure on unstable surface

injuryNotes.lower_back: High core demand from instability. Severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Unstable handles increase rotator cuff demand. Severity ≤3.

purpose: Push-up on unstable handles — dramatically increases core and shoulder stabilizer demand.
whyForYou: Adds instability to pressing — builds the shoulder stabilizers your labrum needs (when cleared).
proTip: These are MUCH harder than they look. Reduce reps significantly when starting.

name: TRX Squat

prerequisites.notes: TRX assists balance — easier than BW squat for some

steps: Hold TRX handles. Lean back slightly.
Squat — TRX helps with balance and assists the ascent.
Depth to comfort. Keep tension on straps.
Drive up through heels. Use less TRX assist over time.

injuryNotes.lower_back: TRX assists balance — less compensatory back strain.
injuryNotes.knee: TRX allows controlled depth. Good for building left knee confidence.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Just holding straps — light demand.

purpose: TRX-assisted squat — excellent for building squat confidence with balance support.
whyForYou: TRX takes the balance component away so you can focus purely on squat mechanics.
proTip: Progressive independence: start pulling on straps, finish barely touching them.

name: TRX Lunge (Rear Foot Suspended)
steps: One foot in TRX stirrup behind you (like BSS).
Descend — front knee tracks over toes.
Drive through front heel to stand.
More unstable than bench BSS — harder.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Upright torso. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: More unstable than bench BSS. Knee severity ≤2. Watch valgus.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact — foot in strap.

purpose: Suspended rear-foot split squat — TRX adds instability to the BSS pattern.
whyForYou: TRX version is more unstable than bench BSS — greater proprioceptive challenge for sport transfer.
proTip: The instability is the feature — it forces more hip stabilizer work than a bench.

name: TRX Pike
steps: Feet in TRX stirrups. Plank position.
Pike hips to ceiling — feet slide toward hands.
Lower controlled to plank.
Body forms inverted V at top.

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED (severity 3 > gate 2). Extreme core demand with spinal flexion component.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Significant demand. Severity ≤3.

purpose: Advanced suspended core — anti-extension with dynamic pike component.
whyForYou: Advanced core goal for when back severity improves.
proTip: Start with TRX knee tucks before pike — same movement, shorter lever arm.

name: TRX Y-Raise
steps: Face anchor. Lean back holding TRX — arms in Y position.
Pull body up — arms stay in Y. Thumbs up.
Squeeze lower traps. Hold 1s.
Lower slowly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No direct concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Y position — moderate demand. Shoulder severity ≤3. Thumbs up protects subacromial space.

purpose: TRX scapular stabilization in Y pattern — bodyweight lower trap training.
whyForYou: Builds lower trap strength critical for your shoulder stability. Adjustable difficulty by angle.
proTip: More upright = easier. More reclined = harder. Adjust angle to your level.

name: TRX Hamstring Curl
steps: Lie on back. Heels in TRX stirrups. Arms at sides.
Bridge hips up — straight line from shoulders to feet.
CURL heels toward glutes. Hips stay elevated.
Extend legs slowly. Hips stay UP throughout.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Bridge position — moderate back demand. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee flexion under load. Severity ≤3. Reduce ROM if uncomfortable.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact — supine.

purpose: Supine hamstring strengthening — TRX provides instability for added challenge.
whyForYou: Builds hamstring strength your back and knee both need, from a supported position.
proTip: Start with bridge-only (no curl) if the full curl is too difficult.

name: TRX Face Pull

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 12 reps with external rotation at end

steps: Face anchor. Lean back. Handles at face height.
Pull elbows BACK and APART — to double bicep pose.
Squeeze rear delts. Hold 1s.
Lower slowly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Same movement as cable face pull. Adjustable angle. Excellent for labrum.

purpose: TRX face pull — home/travel-friendly version of cable face pulls.
whyForYou: Face pulls anywhere you have a TRX. Same mandatory shoulder exercise, portable format.
proTip: Adjust angle to match cable face pull intensity — more reclined = harder.

name: Band Overhead Press
steps: Stand on band. Hold ends at shoulder height.
Press overhead — band gets harder at top (accommodating resistance).
Lower to shoulders. Don't go behind neck.
Keep core braced to prevent arching.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Brace core. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED currently (severity 2 = gate). Overhead pressing. Band accommodating resistance is easier at bottom (less tension) which is the risky position for labrum.

purpose: Band overhead press — accommodating resistance is easier at the bottom where shoulder is most vulnerable.
whyForYou: Future overhead option. Band is gentler than DBs at bottom position — less labrum stress at the start.
proTip: Band resistance increases through the range — less at bottom (where shoulder is vulnerable), more at top.

name: Band Squat
steps: Stand on band. Hold ends at shoulders.
Squat — band provides resistance standing up.
Accommodating resistance — harder at top.
Same form as BW squat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Band adds load — back severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Holding band at shoulders — minimal demand.

purpose: Band squat — adds accommodating resistance to bodyweight squat. Portable loading.
whyForYou: Adds resistance to your squat without needing dumbbells. Take a band anywhere.
proTip: Double the band for more resistance. Or use a heavier band.

name: Band Deadlift
steps: Stand on band, feet hip-width. Hold band ends at thighs.
Hinge at hips — push butt BACK. Band goes slack.
Drive hips forward to stand — band provides resistance.
Back stays FLAT throughout.

breathing.pattern: Inhale on hinge, exhale on drive

injuryNotes.lower_back: Same rules as all hinges. Flat back. Severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Just holding band — no stress.

purpose: Band deadlift — hinge pattern with band resistance. Portable, travel-friendly.
whyForYou: Hinge pattern you can do anywhere with just a band. Outdoor and travel hinge option.
proTip: Loop band around mid-foot and hold at hip crease for the cleanest resistance curve.

name: Band Chest Press
steps: Band around back, under armpits. Hold ends at chest.
Press forward. Band resists the push.
Return slowly — control the band.
Accommodating resistance — harder at extension.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Band is easier at start (less tension where shoulder is vulnerable) — good for labrum. Severity ≤3.

purpose: Band chest press — horizontal push without bench. Accommodating resistance.
whyForYou: Standing chest press with a band. Accommodating resistance is gentler at the shoulder-vulnerable start position.
proTip: Anchor band behind you (doorframe) for consistent line of resistance.

name: Band Lat Pulldown

prerequisites.notes: Pull-apart mastered. Shoulder severity ≤3 for overhead.

steps: Anchor band overhead (door frame, pull-up bar).
Kneel or sit. Grab band wide.
Pull down to chest — elbows drive DOWN.
Return slowly overhead.
formCues: ✅ Pull to CHEST — not behind neck

injuryNotes.lower_back: No direct concern. Seated/kneeling.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Overhead position start. Shoulder severity ≤3. NEVER behind neck. To CHEST only.

purpose: Band lat pulldown — vertical pulling for home training without a machine.
whyForYou: Vertical pulling option at home. Builds lat and upper back strength.
proTip: Anchor high — the higher the anchor, the better the line of pull matches a real pulldown.

name: Band Tricep Pushdown
steps: Anchor band overhead. Grip ends.
Elbows pinned to sides. Push down to full extension.
Squeeze triceps. Return slowly.
Same movement as cable pushdown.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Elbows at sides — minimal shoulder demand.

purpose: Home-friendly tricep pushdown — band replaces cable.
whyForYou: Tricep isolation anywhere you can anchor a band. Supports pressing movements.
proTip: Anchor band in door frame at top. Mimics cable pushdown exactly.

name: Band Hamstring Curl (Prone)
steps: Lie face down. Band around one ankle, anchored at floor level.
Curl heel toward glute against band resistance.
Hold 1s at top. Lower slowly.
Switch legs.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Prone — no spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee flexion against resistance. Severity ≤3. Reduce range if uncomfortable.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Prone hamstring curl with band — isolates hamstrings without machines.
whyForYou: Home-friendly hamstring isolation. Supports your hinge pattern and knee stability.
proTip: Anchor band to a heavy furniture leg or door anchor at floor level.

name: Band Hip Thrust
steps: Back on bench. Band above knees.
Feet flat, under knees.
Drive hips up — squeeze glutes. Push knees OUT against band.
Lower slowly. Chin tucked.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Chin tucked protects spine. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: Band above knees cues abduction — helps knee tracking. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Bench edge at mid-scapula. Pad if needed.

purpose: Hip thrust with band — abduction cue and hip extension resistance combined.
whyForYou: Band adds abduction cue that fires your glute med AND max simultaneously. Two benefits in one.
proTip: The band above knees forces you to push knees out — automatic glute med activation.
source: Bret Contreras hip thrust research, Band training

name: Isometric Wall Push (Chest)

prerequisites.notes: Isometric — zero motion. Safe for acute pain.

steps: Stand facing wall. Hands at shoulder height.
PUSH into wall — 50-70% effort. Hold 5s. No movement.
Relax. Repeat.
Progress effort level as tolerated.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: ZERO motion — safest possible pressing. For acute shoulder pain when even wall push-ups hurt.

purpose: Isometric chest/shoulder pressing — builds strength without joint motion. For acute pain phases.
whyForYou: When your shoulder is too painful for even wall push-ups, this builds strength without any motion.
proTip: Isometrics at specific joint angles build strength at that angle. Try at 3 different arm positions.
source: Isometric training research, Pain management protocols

name: Isometric Squat Hold (Wall Sit)
steps: Back against wall. Slide to comfortable angle.
Hold position — breathe continuously.
Start at shallow angle, progress deeper over weeks.
Target 30s holds.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Wall supports back. No concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Adjustable angle is the key benefit. Start high, go lower as strength builds. Knee severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Isometric squat — builds quad strength without joint motion. Adjustable difficulty by angle.
whyForYou: Adjustable version of VMO wall sit. Start very high and progress deeper.
proTip: Research shows isometric holds at specific angles build strength at those angles. Train at your weakest angle.

name: Isometric Row Hold (Band)
steps: Band anchored in front. Pull to chest — retract blades.
HOLD pulled position for 10s.
Release slowly. Repeat.
Focus on scapular retraction.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Isometric in retracted position — safe for all shoulder conditions.

purpose: Isometric back pulling — builds scapular retraction endurance without motion.
whyForYou: Builds the scapular endurance your posture needs. Isometric holds train endurance specifically.
proTip: Postural muscles need endurance more than strength. Isometric holds train exactly that.
source: Isometric training research, Posture correction protocols

name: Isometric Plank Variations
steps: FRONT plank: elbows and toes (or knees). 15-30s.
RIGHT side plank: bottom elbow and feet (or knees). 15-20s.
LEFT side plank: same. 15-20s.
Cycle through all three. Rest 30s between.
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too long with poor form

injuryNotes.lower_back: Start from knees. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Elbow plank. Severity ≤4. If left shoulder hurts, skip side plank on that side.

purpose: Multi-position isometric core training — front, side, and anti-rotation in one circuit.
whyForYou: McGill recommends endurance planks over crunches. Circuit format builds comprehensive core endurance.
proTip: McGill recommends descending hold times: 10s, 8s, 6s, 4s — not going to failure.

name: Isometric Shoulder Abduction (Wall)

prerequisites.notes: Isometric — zero motion. Safe for acute shoulder.

steps: Stand sideways to wall. Arm at side.
Push outer arm INTO wall — abduction effort. Hold 5s.
No visible movement — pure isometric.
50% effort. Repeat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: ZERO motion — safe even in acute impingement or labrum pain. Builds deltoid strength without ROM.

purpose: Isometric shoulder abduction — builds deltoid strength without any shoulder motion.
whyForYou: When your labrum prevents lateral raises, this builds the same muscles without any movement.
proTip: Try at 3 different arm heights — each angle builds strength specifically at that position.

name: Isometric Hip Adduction (Ball Squeeze)
steps: Sit or lie with a ball (or pillow) between knees.
SQUEEZE knees together — hold 5s.
Release. Repeat.
Engages adductors + pelvic floor.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern — seated or supine.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Isometric adductor and pelvic floor activation — builds inner thigh strength without motion.
whyForYou: Adductor + pelvic floor co-activation. Supports pelvic stability for your back.
proTip: Pillow works if you don't have a ball. Even squeezing a fist between knees works.

name: Leg Swings (Forward/Back + Lateral)
steps: Hold wall for balance. Stand on one leg.
Swing free leg FORWARD and BACK — 10 reps.
Then swing LATERAL (side to side across body) — 10 reps.
Switch legs. Gradually increase swing height.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep torso still. Severity ≤4. If back pain, do cat-cow instead.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Dynamic hip ROM in sagittal and frontal planes — prepares hip joint for exercise.
whyForYou: Opens hip ROM dynamically before your workout. Better than static stretching for warm-up.
proTip: Start small, finish big — gradually increase the swing amplitude over 10 reps.

name: Arm Circles
steps: Stand tall. Arms out to sides.
Small forward circles — 10 reps.
Small backward circles — 10 reps.
Gradually increase circle size.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Start SMALL. Increase gradually. If left shoulder catches at any point, reduce circle size. Severity ≤4.

purpose: Dynamic shoulder warm-up — increases shoulder temperature and synovial fluid.
whyForYou: Simple shoulder warm-up before any upper body work. Start small for your labrum.
proTip: Small to big circles over the set — don't start at full range.

name: Walking Lunges (Bodyweight)
steps: Step forward into lunge. Back knee hovers above floor.
Drive through front heel to step forward into next lunge.
Alternate legs — walking forward.
Upright torso throughout.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Upright torso. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED for your left knee currently (severity 2 = gate). Dynamic lunge demands significant knee control. Use BW squat instead.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Dynamic hip flexor lengthening + glute activation — walking pattern warm-up.
whyForYou: Future warm-up once left knee severity drops. Great dynamic stretch for hip flexors.
proTip: Start with reverse lunges (stepping back) — less knee stress than forward lunges.

name: High Knees (March or Skip)

prerequisites.notes: Walking tolerated. Knee severity ≤2 for impact.

steps: Stand tall. March in place — lift knees to hip height.
Pump arms opposite to legs.
Start with marching, progress to light skip.
20s on, 20s rest.
commonMistakes: ❌ Landing flat-footed (land on balls of feet)

injuryNotes.lower_back: Low-impact march version is safe. Skip version adds impact — severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: Marching = low impact. Skipping = more impact. Knee severity ≤2 for skip version. Use march only if in doubt.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arm pumping optional.

purpose: Dynamic warm-up cardio — raises heart rate and warms hip flexors/quads.
whyForYou: MARCH version is your option. Skip version is future once knee improves. Gets blood flowing fast.
proTip: March version (no skip) is perfectly fine for warm-up — skip is just a progression.

name: Butt Kicks (Walking)
steps: Walk forward (or light jog in place).
With each step, kick heel toward glute.
Opposite arm pumps forward.
Start walking, progress to jogging.
formCues: ✅ Heel to glute with each step

injuryNotes.lower_back: No direct concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Rapid knee flexion. Knee severity ≤2. Walking version only if knee is sensitive.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arm pumping optional.

purpose: Dynamic quad stretch + hamstring activation — common warm-up drill.
whyForYou: Walking version stretches quads dynamically. Jogging version adds cardio component.
proTip: Walking butt kicks = dynamic quad stretch. Jogging = cardio warm-up. Choose your goal.

name: Lateral Shuffle

prerequisites.notes: Walking tolerated. Knee severity ≤2 for lateral movement.

steps: Athletic stance — quarter squat, feet shoulder-width.
Shuffle RIGHT — push off left foot, step right, follow with left.
Don't let feet come together (maintain base).
20 steps right, 20 left.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No direct concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Lateral movement demands. Knee severity ≤2. Use lateral band walk if knee is sensitive.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Lateral movement warm-up — prepares hip abductors/adductors for frontal plane movement.
whyForYou: Frontal plane warm-up critical for surfing, BJJ, and hiking on uneven terrain.
proTip: Arms should be in athletic 'ready position' — as if guarding in basketball.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Dynamic Warm-Up, Sports performance

name: Carioca / Grapevine

prerequisites.notes: Phase 2+. Lateral shuffle mastered. Rotation + lateral demands.

steps: Move laterally. Trail foot crosses IN FRONT, then BEHIND, alternating.
Front cross, side step, back cross, side step — repeat.
Arms swing naturally for counter-rotation.
Keep hips and shoulders facing forward.
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too fast before coordination develops

injuryNotes.lower_back: Rotation component — severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Rotational hip warm-up + coordination — lateral movement with cross-stepping.
whyForYou: Builds lateral coordination and hip rotation — directly transfers to BJJ and surfing.
proTip: Start SLOW. Speed comes after coordination. This is one of the best athletic warm-ups.

name: Toy Soldiers (Straight Leg Kicks)
steps: Walk forward. With each step, kick straight leg up to opposite hand.
Reach hand toward rising foot — don't round back to reach.
Alternate legs with each step.
Gradually increase kick height.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Dynamic hamstring stretch involves hip flexion — severity ≤3. Don't round forward to reach foot.
injuryNotes.knee: Straight leg — no flexion stress. Knee severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Reaching hand adds a tiny shoulder component — negligible.

purpose: Dynamic hamstring stretch — walking pattern with straight-leg kicks.
whyForYou: Dynamic hamstring stretching before your workout — better than static for warm-up.
proTip: Reach toward the foot, but don't round your back to get there. Height comes with time.

name: Ladder Quick Feet

prerequisites.notes: Phase 2+. Knee severity ≤2. Lateral shuffle mastered.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full ladder both directions without mistakes

steps: Place ladder on ground. Start at one end.
Two feet in each box — quick, light steps.
Move forward through entire ladder.
Variations: lateral, in-out, icky shuffle.
formCues: ✅ Light on feet — balls of feet only

injuryNotes.lower_back: No direct concern. Low load.
injuryNotes.knee: Quick foot contacts. Knee severity ≤2. Not plyometric but fast pace demands knee stability.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Foot speed and coordination — builds neuromuscular quickness for sport.
whyForYou: Foot speed for surfing pop-ups, BJJ footwork, and hiking on uneven terrain.
proTip: Quality over speed initially. Clean patterns first, then add speed.

name: Cone Shuffle Drill
steps: Set 4-5 cones in a line, 5 feet apart.
Shuffle laterally between cones — touch each one.
Shuffle back. That's one rep.
Stay low, athletic stance throughout.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Lateral agility — change of direction speed and hip stabilizer strength.
whyForYou: Change of direction training for BJJ, surfing transitions, and general athleticism.
proTip: Deceleration is where injuries happen. Focus on STOPPING cleanly at each cone.

name: Box Drill (4-Corner)
steps: 4 cones in a 10x10 foot square.
Sprint forward to cone 1. Shuffle RIGHT to cone 2.
Backpedal to cone 3. Shuffle LEFT back to start.
That's one rep. Reverse direction next rep.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Multi-directional — severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Multi-directional agility — forward, lateral, and backward movement in one drill.
whyForYou: Multi-directional speed for BJJ, surfing, and general athletic performance.
proTip: The backpedal is the hardest part for most people. Practice that segment separately.

name: Reaction Ball Catches
steps: Throw reaction ball at wall (or bounce on floor).
React and catch — ball bounces unpredictably.
Stay in athletic stance. Quick hands.
30s rounds, 3 rounds.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Low demand — just reactions.
injuryNotes.knee: Athletic stance with quick reactions. Knee severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Quick reaching. Severity ≤4.

purpose: Hand-eye coordination + reflexes — unpredictable stimulus forces reactive movement.
whyForYou: Reaction time for BJJ, surfing (pop-up), and general coordination.
proTip: A regular tennis ball works if you don't have a reaction ball.

name: Single-Leg Hop and Stick

prerequisites.notes: Phase 3+. Knee severity ≤1 (BLOCKED for John). Plyometric readiness required.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Stick landings consistently with zero valgus

steps: Single leg. Hop in different directions — forward, lateral, diagonal.
LAND on same leg. STICK 3s.
Zero knee valgus on landing.
If knee caves at ALL — too advanced.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Impact — severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED (severity 2 > gate 1). Plyometric landing. Future exercise after full knee recovery.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Multi-directional single-leg plyometric — tests landing mechanics in all planes.
whyForYou: Advanced plyometric goal for when knee is fully recovered. Tests complete athletic readiness.
proTip: Stick = freeze. If you can't freeze the landing, the hop was too big.

name: T-Drill
steps: Set cones in T shape (10 yd forward, 5 yd each side).
Sprint to center cone. Shuffle LEFT to cone. Shuffle RIGHT past center to far cone.
Shuffle back to center. Backpedal to start.
That's one rep.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Multi-directional with sprint. Severity ≤4.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Combined forward/lateral/backward agility — standard athletic assessment and training drill.
whyForYou: The gold standard agility test. When you can do this clean, your athleticism is back.
proTip: Time yourself. Track progress. Good T-drill time is sport-specific but under 10s is excellent.

name: Plate Pinch Hold
steps: Pinch a weight plate between thumb and fingers. Smooth side out.
Hold at your side. Don't let it slip.
Hold 15-20s. Set down controlled.
Progress plate weight over time.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arm at side — minimal demand.

purpose: Pinch grip strength — thumb/finger opposition. Supports deadlift and carry grip.
whyForYou: Builds the pinch grip that supports your deadlifts and carries.
proTip: 2 plates smooth-side-out is the classic pinch grip test. Track max weight held for 10s.

name: Dead Hang (Pull-Up Bar)

prerequisites.notes: Grip base. Shoulder must tolerate full overhead hang.

steps: Grab pull-up bar — shoulder width, overhand grip.
Hang with arms fully extended. Feet off floor.
RELAX — let shoulders stretch under bodyweight.
Hold as long as possible. Track time.

injuryNotes.lower_back: EXCELLENT for spine — passive decompression. Many back patients report relief from hanging.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Full overhead position. Shoulder severity ≤3. This CAN decompress the shoulder (Kirsch method) but labrum must tolerate the position. If pinching or pain, STOP.

purpose: Grip endurance + shoulder/spine decompression — hanging stretches everything.
whyForYou: Decompresses your spine (great for your back) while building grip. Shoulder must tolerate it.
proTip: The Kirsch method suggests 30s daily hangs prevent most shoulder surgery. But only if tolerated.
source: Kirsch J. Shoulder Pain?, Grip training protocols

name: Wrist Roller
steps: Hold wrist roller at arm's length (or at sides for less shoulder demand).
Roll weight UP by rotating wrists — one hand then the other.
At top, roll DOWN slowly — controlled unwinding.
Do both directions — flexor dominant and extensor dominant.
formCues: ✅ Arms can be extended or at sides

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms extended version requires shoulder endurance. Keep at sides if shoulder is bothered. Severity ≤4.

purpose: Complete forearm training — wrist flexors AND extensors in one exercise.
whyForYou: Builds the forearm balance that supports your grip for deadlifts and carries.
proTip: Roll up = flexors. Roll down = extensors. Both directions for balanced forearms.

name: Rice Bucket Exercises

prerequisites.notes: Need a bucket of rice — that's it

steps: Fill a 5-gallon bucket halfway with uncooked rice.
Plunge hand in. Open/close, rotate, grab, twist, spread fingers.
Cycle through different hand positions — 30s each.
3-5 minutes total per hand.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hand at side — no shoulder demand.

purpose: Comprehensive hand and wrist rehab — works every muscle in the hand and forearm in all directions.
whyForYou: Climbers, BJJ athletes, and surfers all use rice buckets. Builds complete hand/forearm resilience.
proTip: Used by baseball pitchers, rock climbers, and martial artists. $5 of rice and a bucket = complete hand gym.
source: Sports medicine, Baseball pitching rehab, Rock climbing training

name: Gentle Water Walking

prerequisites.notes: For fibromyalgia, chronic pain, severe deconditioning. WARM water preferred.

steps: Enter WARM pool (if available). Chest depth.
Walk gently — short steps, comfortable pace.
Start with 5 min. Add 1-2 min per session.
NO pushing through pain or fatigue.

injuryNotes.lower_back: WARM water reduces pain sensitivity. Buoyancy removes spinal load. Best option for severe pain days.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms at sides — no demand.

purpose: Warm water therapy — gold standard for fibromyalgia, chronic pain, and severe deconditioning.
whyForYou: For your worst pain days. Warm water reduces pain sensitivity while maintaining movement.
proTip: Warm water (88-92°F) reduces pain sensitivity and muscle guarding. It's therapeutic, not just exercise.

name: Restorative Yoga Sequence
steps: Choose 3-5 supported poses: child's pose, legs up wall, supine twist, supported bridge, savasana.
Hold each 1-2 min. Use pillows/blocks for support.
Focus on breathing — 4 in, 6 out.
This is REST, not stretching.
formCues: ✅ Zero effort — let supports hold you

injuryNotes.lower_back: All poses supported. Excellent for pain management. Modify any pose that bothers back.
injuryNotes.knee: Props under knees in any pose. Avoid deep flexion.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Support arms with pillows. No weight-bearing on shoulders.

purpose: Parasympathetic activation through supported resting poses — for pain management and recovery.
whyForYou: Active recovery and pain management. Your nervous system needs rest as much as your muscles.
proTip: Legs up the wall for 5 min is the single best restorative pose. Try it daily.

name: Progressive Muscle Relaxation

prerequisites.notes: No prerequisites — for stress and pain management

steps: Lie on back. Close eyes. Start with FEET.
TENSE the muscle group hard for 5s. Then RELEASE completely for 10s.
Move UP the body: feet → calves → thighs → glutes → abdomen → chest → hands → arms → shoulders → face.
Notice the contrast between tension and relaxation.
formCues: ✅ Move systematically from feet to head
✅ Notice the release — that's the therapy
coreBracing: Varies — each muscle group tensed then released.

breathing.pattern: Inhale during tense, long exhale during release

injuryNotes.lower_back: Skip abdominal tensing if it causes back pain. Very gentle overall.
injuryNotes.knee: Tense quads gently if knee is sensitive.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Tense shoulders gently — 50% effort max.

purpose: Jacobson's Progressive Muscle Relaxation — evidence-based stress and pain management technique.
whyForYou: Pain creates muscle guarding → more pain. PMR breaks that cycle. Essential for chronic pain management.
proTip: The RELEASE is the therapy, not the tension. Focus your attention on how the release feels.
source: Jacobson E. Progressive Relaxation, Pain management research

name: Big Step Walking (LSVT BIG Concept)

prerequisites.notes: For Parkinson's or anyone who needs movement amplitude training

steps: Walk with EXAGGERATED movements — BIG steps, BIG arm swings.
Lift knees higher than normal. Swing arms wider.
Think 'taking up space' with every movement.
This is amplitude-based training — BIGGER is better.
commonMistakes: ❌ Feeling silly and stopping (this IS the exercise)

injuryNotes.lower_back: Exaggerated movements are low load but increase ROM. No concern if walking is tolerated.
injuryNotes.knee: Big steps increase knee ROM demand. Knee severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Big arm swings — no significant shoulder load.

purpose: Amplitude-based movement training — LSVT BIG concept for movement size restoration.
whyForYou: Amplitude training isn't just for Parkinson's — it improves general movement quality and confidence.
proTip: Feel ridiculous? Good. That means you're doing it big enough. It works because it recalibrates your movement sense.
source: LSVT BIG Program, Parkinson's exercise research

name: Dual-Task Walking

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 5 min walking + cognitive task without stopping

steps: Walk at comfortable pace.
Add a cognitive task: count backward from 100 by 7s, name animals A-Z, recite months backward.
Maintain walking speed AND cognitive task simultaneously.
If walking slows or stops — that's your current limit.
commonMistakes: ❌ Only doing cognitive OR motor (must be both)

breathing.pattern: Continuous — don't hold breath while thinking

injuryNotes.lower_back: Regular walking — no added concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Cognitive-motor dual-task training — trains the brain to handle multiple demands simultaneously.
whyForYou: Falls often happen when attention is divided. Training dual-task reduces your risk during real-world activities.
proTip: This is why elderly people fall when talking and walking — they haven't trained the dual-task. You're training it now.

name: Boxing Drills (Seated or Standing)

prerequisites.notes: Accessible for neurological populations. Seated version available for wheelchair users.

steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, or sit upright in a sturdy chair.
Bring fists up by chin, elbows tucked at your sides.
Throw alternating jabs, crosses, and hooks with controlled speed — fully extend but don't lock elbows.
Rotate your torso with each punch to engage core.
formCues: ✅ Keep guard up between punches — fists at chin height
✅ Rotate torso with each cross and hook
✅ Stay light on feet, weight slightly forward
commonMistakes: ❌ Locking elbows on full extension — hyperextension risk
❌ Holding breath — exhale sharply with each punch
coreBracing: LIGHT (20-30%) — engage core through natural rotation, brace on impact

breathing.pattern: Sharp exhale with each punch, inhale between combos

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated version eliminates spinal rotation under load. Stand version: keep punches compact, avoid wild hooks.
injuryNotes.knee: Seated version removes all knee demand. Standing: stay flat-footed, no bouncing.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep punches below shoulder height if labrum is sensitive. Avoid wide hooks. Stop if clicking or catching.

purpose: Coordination, speed, and dopamine activation — evidence-based for Parkinson's disease management (Rock Steady Boxing).
whyForYou: Cardio without lower body impact. Seated option if energy is low. Great stress release on high-stress days.
proTip: Focus on speed and snap, not power. Imagine snapping a towel — fast out, fast back.
source: Rock Steady Boxing / LSVT BIG principles

name: Affected Arm Passive ROM

prerequisites.notes: Post-stroke rehab. Affected arm guided by unaffected arm to prevent contracture.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free with full passive ROM

steps: Sit or lie comfortably. Use your unaffected hand to hold the wrist of the affected arm.
Slowly raise the affected arm overhead (flexion) as far as comfortable — hold 2s at end range.
Lower slowly. Repeat for abduction (out to side) and external rotation.
Perform 10 reps in each direction, twice daily.
formCues: ✅ Move slowly and gently — let the unaffected arm do the work
✅ Support the wrist and elbow to prevent hanging
✅ Stop at first sign of pain or resistance
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing the arm past its available range
❌ Jerking or bouncing at end range
❌ Forgetting to breathe — tension increases spasticity
coreBracing: MINIMAL (10%) — focus is on relaxation of the affected arm

breathing.pattern: Inhale at rest, exhale during the movement to promote relaxation

injuryNotes.lower_back: Perform seated or supine to avoid standing fatigue.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement. Seated or supine.
injuryNotes.shoulder: If labrum tear is on the affected side, limit abduction to 90 degrees and avoid end-range external rotation.

purpose: Prevent contracture and maintain joint ROM in the affected arm after stroke. Repetitive passive motion supports neuroplasticity.
whyForYou: Applicable shoulder ROM principles — gentle passive motion is also beneficial for your labrum recovery on low-energy days.
proTip: Do this in a warm shower — heat relaxes muscles and allows greater ROM.
source: Stroke rehabilitation guidelines / Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy principles

name: Bilateral Arm Training (Mirror Movements)

prerequisites.notes: Post-stroke rehab. Both arms move simultaneously to leverage neuroplasticity.

steps: Sit upright. Extend both arms in front of you at chest height.
Perform matching movements with both arms simultaneously — flexion, abduction, or elbow curls.
Let the unaffected arm lead; the affected arm mirrors as closely as possible.
Complete 10 reps per pattern. Focus on symmetry, not range.
formCues: ✅ Both arms move at the same speed — match the slower side
✅ Sit tall with shoulders relaxed, not shrugged
commonMistakes: ❌ Letting the strong arm dominate speed and range
❌ Tensing the neck and upper traps — relax the jaw
❌ Giving up on the affected side — even small movements count
coreBracing: LIGHT (15-20%) — enough to sit upright, not more

breathing.pattern: Exhale during the movement, inhale on return

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated position is safe. Use a back-supported chair.
injuryNotes.shoulder: If labrum is involved, limit range to pain-free arc. Avoid end-range overhead.

purpose: Bilateral training activates motor cortex on both sides simultaneously, enhancing neuroplasticity after stroke.
whyForYou: Mirror training principles can help your shoulder rehab — the strong side teaches the injured side.
proTip: Use a mirror between your arms so your brain sees the affected arm moving perfectly — this is called mirror therapy.
source: Bilateral arm training studies / Mirror therapy (Ramachandran)

name: Sit-to-Stand Practice

prerequisites.notes: Fundamental functional movement. Critical for post-stroke independence.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps without arm support, controlled 3s each way

steps: Sit on a sturdy chair with feet flat, hip-width apart, knees over ankles.
Lean forward from the hips — nose over toes — and press through your heels to stand.
Stand fully upright, squeeze glutes at the top.
Slowly lower back to the chair over 3 seconds — control the descent, don't plop.
formCues: ✅ Lean forward before standing — shift weight to feet first
✅ Keep chest up and spine neutral throughout
commonMistakes: ❌ Using momentum or rocking to stand — control the movement
❌ Knees caving inward (valgus) — push knees out over toes
❌ Dropping onto the chair on the way down — lower slowly
coreBracing: MODERATE (30%) — brace core to keep trunk stable during the transition

breathing.pattern: Inhale seated, exhale as you stand. Inhale at top, exhale as you lower.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep spine neutral. Use a higher chair to reduce hip flexion demand. Use arms on armrests for assist if needed.
injuryNotes.knee: Use a higher seat to limit knee flexion. Press through heels. Watch for left knee valgus.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement. Arms can be crossed on chest or used for balance.

purpose: Functional independence — the ability to stand from a chair is the #1 predictor of independence in older adults and post-stroke populations.
whyForYou: Builds foundational squat-pattern strength without load. Good warm-up for knee and back on low-energy days.
proTip: Add a 3-second pause at the bottom to eliminate momentum — pure strength.
source: Post-stroke rehabilitation guidelines / Functional movement assessment

name: Pursed Lip Breathing with Movement

prerequisites.notes: COPD primary breathing technique. Safe for all populations.

steps: Sit or stand comfortably. Relax your shoulders and neck.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 2 counts — feel belly expand.
Purse your lips as if blowing through a straw. Exhale slowly for 4 counts.
Pair with gentle arm raises or walking once the pattern is comfortable.
formCues: ✅ Exhale should be TWICE as long as inhale
✅ Keep shoulders down and relaxed — breathe into belly, not chest
commonMistakes: ❌ Exhaling too fast — the slow exhale is the therapeutic component
❌ Tensing neck and shoulders during exhale

breathing.pattern: Nose inhale 2s, pursed lip exhale 4s. Ratio is key: 1:2.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated position recommended. This breathing pattern supports core stabilization.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep arms relaxed at sides if shoulder is sensitive.

purpose: Improves breathing efficiency by maintaining positive airway pressure, preventing airway collapse in COPD. Also excellent for stress management.
whyForYou: Great pre-workout breathing drill. Activates diaphragm and parasympathetic system — calms stress before training.
proTip: Use this technique during rest periods between sets to accelerate recovery and lower heart rate.
source: ACSM Guidelines for COPD / Pulmonary Rehabilitation Standards

name: Upper Body Ergometer (Arm Bike)

prerequisites.notes: Cardio without lower body demand. Ideal for COPD, lower limb injuries, wheelchair users.

steps: Adjust seat height so handles are at chest level. Sit upright with feet flat.
Grip handles, start pedaling forward with a smooth, circular motion.
Maintain steady cadence — 40-60 RPM. Increase resistance gradually.
After 5-15 minutes, reverse direction for 2-3 minutes to balance muscles.
formCues: ✅ Sit tall — no slouching or leaning into the machine
✅ Smooth circular motion — no jerky pulling
commonMistakes: ❌ Hunching shoulders up to ears — relax them down
❌ Gripping too tight — light grip reduces forearm fatigue
❌ Going too fast with poor form — steady cadence over speed
coreBracing: LIGHT (15-20%) — maintain upright posture without rigid bracing

breathing.pattern: Rhythmic breathing matched to cadence. Pursed lip exhale for COPD patients.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated position with back support is safe. No spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement — excellent lower-body-free cardio option.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Monitor for labrum clicking. Reduce range if impingement occurs. Keep resistance low initially.

purpose: Cardiovascular conditioning without lower body demand. Primary cardio modality for COPD, wheelchair users, and lower limb injuries.
whyForYou: Knee-friendly cardio option. Use on days when your knee is flared up but you still want conditioning work.
proTip: Reverse pedaling targets the posterior chain (rear delts, rhomboids) — do 30% of your time in reverse.

name: Post-Meal Walking (10-15 min)

prerequisites.notes: Primary glucose management tool. 10-15 min light walking within 30 min of eating.

steps: Within 15-30 minutes of finishing a meal, begin walking at a comfortable pace.
Maintain an easy, conversational pace — you should be able to talk normally.
Walk for 10-15 minutes. Indoors (treadmill/hallway) or outdoors.
Focus on posture: tall spine, relaxed shoulders, natural arm swing.
formCues: ✅ Walk at a pace where you can hold a conversation easily
✅ Stand tall — don't slouch or look down at phone
✅ Land heel-to-toe with a natural stride
commonMistakes: ❌ Walking too fast — this is NOT a workout, it's glucose management
❌ Waiting too long after eating — within 30 min is most effective
❌ Skipping it after large meals — this is when it matters most

breathing.pattern: Natural relaxed breathing. Nasal breathing preferred.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Gentle walking supports disc nutrition. Keep it flat terrain.
injuryNotes.knee: Flat ground only. Slow pace. If knee is flared, seated marching is an alternative.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No involvement. Let arms swing naturally.

purpose: Post-meal walking reduces blood glucose spikes by 30-50%. Most impactful single health habit for diabetes management.
whyForYou: Adds daily movement without fatigue. Supports recovery between training sessions and improves metabolic health.
proTip: After your largest meal of the day is the most impactful time. Even 5 minutes helps if 15 feels like too much.
source: ACSM Diabetes Guidelines / Diabetes Care journal meta-analysis

name: Circuit Training Template

prerequisites.notes: Alternating upper/lower body exercises with minimal rest. Excellent metabolic conditioning for diabetes.

steps: Select 4-6 exercises alternating upper and lower body (e.g., squat, push-up, lunge, row, glute bridge, plank).
Perform 10-15 reps of each exercise back-to-back with only 15-30s rest between.
After completing all exercises (1 round), rest 60-90s.
Repeat for 2-3 total rounds. Adjust exercise selection to your injury profile.
formCues: ✅ Maintain form quality even when fatigued — slow down rather than get sloppy
✅ Monitor breathing — don't hold breath between exercises
commonMistakes: ❌ Sacrificing form for speed — quality over pace
❌ Choosing exercises that are too complex for a fatigued state
❌ Skipping the rest between rounds — the recovery matters
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — maintain brace throughout circuit, especially on transitions

breathing.pattern: Exhale on exertion for each exercise. Deep breaths during transitions.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Exclude deadlifts and loaded flexion. Use glute bridges, pallof press, bird dog instead.
injuryNotes.knee: Replace lunges with glute bridges. Limit squat depth. No jumping movements.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Replace overhead pressing with landmine press. Include face pulls as one station.

purpose: Metabolic conditioning that improves insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake. Alternating muscle groups keeps heart rate elevated.
whyForYou: Efficient full-body conditioning when time is limited. Adapts to your injuries by swapping individual exercises.
proTip: Place your hardest exercises first when you're freshest. End with core or carries.
source: ACSM Diabetes Exercise Guidelines / NSCA Circuit Training Protocols

name: Modified Plank (Incline/Knees)

prerequisites.notes: Pregnancy-safe core training. Incline version avoids supine position after 1st trimester.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 30s hold with perfect form, no low back sagging

steps: Place hands on elevated surface (bench/wall) or set up on knees on the floor.
Walk feet back until body forms a straight line from head to knees (or heels if incline).
Squeeze glutes, brace core at 40%. Hold position — do not let hips sag or pike.
