Breathe normally throughout the hold. Release when form breaks.
formCues: ✅ Body forms ONE straight line — no sagging or piking
✅ Squeeze glutes to protect the lower back
✅ Elbows or hands directly under shoulders
commonMistakes: ❌ Hips dropping toward the floor — core disengagement
❌ Looking up or forward — keep neck neutral, eyes at floor
coreBracing: MODERATE (40%) — maintain steady brace throughout hold

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally through the hold. Don't hold your breath.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Use incline version (hands on bench) to reduce load. Stop if any low back pain.
injuryNotes.knee: Pad knees if on-knees version. Incline full plank avoids knee pressure entirely.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Use forearms instead of hands if wrist/shoulder discomfort. Reduce hold time.

purpose: Core stabilization without supine position — safe during pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester) and for anyone who cannot lie flat.
whyForYou: Lower-load plank variant for your post-surgical back. Use incline to scale difficulty perfectly to your capacity.
proTip: The wall plank is the easiest version — start there and progressively lower the surface height over weeks.
source: ACOG Exercise During Pregnancy Guidelines / NASM OPT Phase 1

name: Pelvic Floor Kegels (Progressive)

prerequisites.notes: Pregnancy and postpartum foundational exercise. Also benefits everyone for core stability.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Able to isolate PF without bearing down

steps: Sit, stand, or lie down comfortably. Relax your abdomen, thighs, and buttocks.
Imagine stopping the flow of urine — gently lift and squeeze the pelvic floor muscles.
Hold for 3-5 seconds (progress to 10 seconds over weeks). Fully relax for equal time.
Perform 10 repetitions, 3 times per day. Quality over quantity.
formCues: ✅ Isolate the pelvic floor — no squeezing glutes or inner thighs
✅ The release is as important as the contraction — fully relax between reps
✅ Breathe normally throughout — don't hold your breath
commonMistakes: ❌ Bearing down instead of lifting up — this is the opposite of a Kegel
❌ Squeezing glutes and thighs — only the pelvic floor should engage
❌ Holding breath — this increases intra-abdominal pressure
coreBracing: MINIMAL — isolate pelvic floor only, do not globally brace

breathing.pattern: Exhale gently during contraction, inhale during relaxation

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine position may be most comfortable. PF engagement supports spinal stabilization.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Strengthens the pelvic floor to prevent/treat incontinence, support pregnancy, and improve core stability.
whyForYou: Pelvic floor is the base of your core canister. Strengthening it improves your dead bug, plank, and all bracing exercises.
proTip: Try cueing 'lift your pelvic floor like an elevator going up floors 1-2-3' to find the right muscles.
source: ACOG Pregnancy Guidelines / Pelvic Floor Physical Therapy Standards

name: Side-Lying Clamshell (Pregnancy-Safe)

prerequisites.notes: Pregnancy-safe hip strengthening. Side-lying avoids supine position. Safe throughout all trimesters.

steps: Lie on your side with knees bent to 45 degrees, hips stacked, head resting on bottom arm.
Keep feet together. Slowly rotate the top knee upward like opening a clamshell.
Hold at the top for 1 second — feel the glute engage, not the hip flexor.
Lower slowly with control. Complete all reps, then switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Hips stay stacked — do NOT roll backward to get more range
✅ Feel the contraction deep in the side of the glute, not the front of the hip
✅ Keep core gently engaged to prevent trunk rotation
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling the pelvis backward to cheat more range — keep hips stacked
❌ Using momentum — slow and controlled wins
❌ Only working one side — do both sides even if one feels easier
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — enough to prevent trunk rotation

breathing.pattern: Exhale as knee opens, inhale as it closes

injuryNotes.lower_back: Side-lying is a spine-neutral position. Place a pillow between knees if uncomfortable.
injuryNotes.knee: If knee pain occurs with rotation, reduce range of motion. Pillow between knees for support.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Bottom arm may compress shoulder — use a pillow under your head.

purpose: Strengthens hip abductors to prevent knee valgus, support pelvic stability during pregnancy, and reduce low back pain.
whyForYou: Your left knee valgus tendency needs strong hip abductors. This exercise targets the exact muscles that prevent knee caving.
proTip: Place a mini band just above your knees for progressive resistance. Start without band, add when 20 reps is easy.
source: ACOG Pregnancy Exercise Guidelines / NASM Corrective Exercise

name: Joint-Specific ROM Cycling

prerequisites.notes: Daily ROM maintenance for osteoarthritis. Move every affected joint through its full available range.

steps: Start at the neck and work downward: neck circles, shoulder circles, elbow bends, wrist circles.
Continue to hips: hip circles (standing or seated), knee bends, ankle circles.
Move each joint through its FULL available range in both directions — 10 reps each way.
Spend extra time on joints that feel stiff. Apply gentle heat beforehand if available.
formCues: ✅ Move slowly and smoothly through the full available range
✅ Stop at pain, not through pain — work to the edge, not past it
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing range of motion through pain — this worsens OA inflammation
❌ Skipping joints that feel 'fine' — maintenance prevents future stiffness
❌ Rushing through the movement — slow, deliberate circles are therapeutic

breathing.pattern: Breathe naturally. Exhale on the tightest portion of each circle.

injuryNotes.lower_back: For spine: use cat-cow instead of lumbar circles. Avoid full spinal rotation under load.
injuryNotes.knee: Move knee through available range only — never force past pain. Seated version reduces load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Small circles first, gradually increase. Avoid end-range if labrum is catching.

purpose: Daily ROM cycling lubricates joints, maintains range of motion, and reduces morning stiffness in osteoarthritis.
whyForYou: Great morning ritual for your left knee and left shoulder. Use as a daily check-in — are things getting better or worse?
proTip: Do this in a warm shower — heat + movement is the best combination for stiff joints.
source: ACSM Osteoarthritis Guidelines / Arthritis Foundation Exercise Recommendations

name: Isometric Quad Set

prerequisites.notes: Gold standard for knee OA. Strengthens quads without joint motion — zero stress on cartilage.

steps: Sit with legs extended or lie supine. Place a small rolled towel under the knee for slight bend.
Press the back of your knee DOWN into the floor or towel — straighten the leg fully.
You should see the quad muscle tighten and the kneecap pull upward slightly.
Hold 5-10 seconds. Relax completely. Repeat 10 times per set.
formCues: ✅ Focus on the VMO (teardrop muscle above inner knee) — it should visibly contract
✅ Press firmly but not maximally — 60-70% effort
✅ Keep foot relaxed — don't point or flex the ankle
commonMistakes: ❌ Not fully engaging the quad — you should SEE the muscle contract
❌ Holding breath during the hold — breathe normally
❌ Skipping the full relaxation between reps — the release matters
coreBracing: MINIMAL (10%) — relaxed, focus entirely on the quad

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally throughout. Don't hold breath.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine position is safe. No spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: This is a PRIMARY knee rehab exercise. Zero joint motion = zero cartilage stress. Do daily.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No involvement.

purpose: Quadriceps strengthening without joint motion — the single most protective factor against knee OA progression.
whyForYou: Your left knee needs VMO strengthening daily. This is the safest quad exercise possible — use it as pre-activation before every session.
proTip: Place your fingers on the VMO (inner knee) to feel it contract. If you can't feel it, you're not engaging it correctly.
source: ACSM Osteoarthritis Guidelines / Knee OA Clinical Practice Guidelines

name: Seated Cat-Cow

prerequisites.notes: Chair yoga — spinal mobility while seated. Accessible for wheelchair users.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Pain-free seated, ready for hands-and-knees version

steps: Sit upright in a chair, feet flat on floor, hands on thighs.
COW: Inhale — arch your back, lift chest, look up slightly. Open shoulders.
CAT: Exhale — round your spine, tuck chin, draw belly button in.
Flow between positions with your breath. One breath per transition.
formCues: ✅ Initiate from the pelvis — tilt pelvis forward for cow, backward for cat
✅ Move through the ENTIRE spine segment by segment
✅ Keep feet grounded and weight even on both sit bones
commonMistakes: ❌ Only moving the head and neck — the whole spine should flow
❌ Holding breath — breath drives the movement
❌ Rushing — slow, intentional movement gets better results
coreBracing: LIGHT (15%) — gentle engagement, focus on mobility not stability

breathing.pattern: Inhale into cow (extension), exhale into cat (flexion)

injuryNotes.lower_back: Move within pain-free range only. Reduce the arc if extension or flexion hurts. This is gentle segmental motion.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hands rest on thighs — no shoulder load.

purpose: Gentle spinal mobility and segmental motion. Warm-up for any seated population including wheelchair users.
whyForYou: Your post-surgical back benefits from daily gentle spinal motion. This is the safest way to warm up your spine.
proTip: Close your eyes and visualize each vertebra moving independently — you'll feel more segments unlock.
source: Yoga Alliance Adaptive Yoga / Chair Yoga for Seniors

name: Seated Forward Fold

prerequisites.notes: Chair yoga. Gentle hamstring and back stretch in seated position.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Comfortable reaching past knees without pain

steps: Sit on the edge of the chair, feet flat, legs hip-width apart.
Hinge forward from the hips — NOT by rounding the lower back.
Let your hands slide down your legs or hang toward the floor.
Hold 20-30 seconds. Breathe deeply. Rise slowly using your hands on thighs.
formCues: ✅ Hinge from the hips first — maintain a long spine as long as possible
✅ Let gravity do the work — don't force the stretch
✅ Relax the neck — let the head hang naturally
commonMistakes: ❌ Rounding the lower back aggressively — lead with the chest, not the head
❌ Bouncing at the bottom — hold steady
❌ Holding breath — long exhales deepen the stretch naturally

breathing.pattern: Long exhales relax the nervous system and deepen the stretch naturally

injuryNotes.lower_back: If disc herniation history: hinge from hips only, keep lower back flat. Avoid if acute flexion intolerance.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee stress in seated version.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms hang passively — no shoulder load.

purpose: Hamstring and back stretch in seated position. Promotes relaxation and parasympathetic activation.
whyForYou: Your tight hamstrings contribute to lower back strain. This gentle seated stretch is safe for your back when done with hip hinge form.
proTip: Place your hands on your shins and maintain a flat back for the first 10 seconds, then let the rounding happen naturally.
source: Yoga Alliance Adaptive Yoga / Chair Yoga for Seniors

name: Seated Spinal Twist

prerequisites.notes: Chair yoga. Thoracic rotation stretch. Gentle spinal twist in seated position.

steps: Sit tall in a chair, feet flat on the floor, spine long.
Place your right hand on the outside of your left knee. Left hand behind you on the chair back.
Inhale to lengthen the spine. Exhale to gently twist left — look over your left shoulder.
Hold 20-30 seconds. Return to center. Repeat on the other side.
formCues: ✅ Grow tall BEFORE you twist — length first, rotation second
✅ Twist from the mid-back (thoracic), not the lower back
✅ Keep both hips grounded on the chair — don't let one lift
commonMistakes: ❌ Twisting from the lower back — the lumbar spine has very little rotation
❌ Forcing the twist with your arm — let the exhale deepen the rotation naturally
❌ Slouching before twisting — sit tall first
coreBracing: LIGHT (15%) — gentle engagement to maintain upright posture

breathing.pattern: Inhale to lengthen, exhale to deepen the twist. Each breath should add a little more rotation.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep rotation in the thoracic spine ONLY. If lower back pain occurs, reduce range. Avoid if acute disc issue.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Don't pull hard with the hand on knee. Gentle guidance only.

purpose: Thoracic rotation and spinal mobility in a safe seated position. Decompresses the spine after sitting.
whyForYou: Your thoracic spine needs rotation work to avoid compensating through the lower back. This is the safest way to train rotation.
proTip: Each exhale should take you slightly deeper into the twist — don't force it on the inhale.
source: Yoga Alliance Adaptive Yoga / Chair Yoga for Seniors

name: Seated Eagle Arms

prerequisites.notes: Chair yoga. Stretches posterior shoulder and upper back. Modify for shoulder injuries.

steps: Sit tall. Extend arms forward at shoulder height.
Cross right arm UNDER left. Bend elbows and try to bring palms together (or backs of hands).
Lift elbows slightly and press forearms forward — feel the stretch across your upper back.
Hold 20-30 seconds. Unwind and repeat with left arm under right.
formCues: ✅ Lift elbows to shoulder height to maximize the stretch
✅ Press forearms away from your face to deepen the stretch
✅ Keep shoulders down away from ears
commonMistakes: ❌ Shrugging shoulders up — actively pull them down
❌ Forcing palms together if flexibility isn't there yet — back of hands touching is fine
❌ Holding breath — breathe into the stretch

breathing.pattern: Breathe into the stretch between your shoulder blades. Exhale to relax deeper.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No spinal load. Safe in seated position.
injuryNotes.shoulder: If labrum catches or clicks, use cross-body stretch instead. Avoid full wrap if painful.

purpose: Stretches posterior shoulder, upper back, and space between shoulder blades. Counteracts forward-rounded posture.
whyForYou: Your left labrum needs careful stretching — use the modified version (backs of hands) to avoid impingement.
proTip: The deeper wrap creates more stretch. If you can't get palms together, hold a strap between your hands.
source: Yoga Alliance Adaptive Yoga / Chair Yoga for Seniors

name: Kegel Holds (Progressive)

prerequisites.notes: Progressive pelvic floor endurance. Start at 3s holds, build to 10s over weeks.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 x 10s holds with full relaxation between

steps: Sit, lie, or stand comfortably. Relax everything except the pelvic floor.
Slowly contract the pelvic floor — imagine lifting an elevator. Hold for 3 seconds (progress to 5, then 10).
Fully release for equal time — the relaxation phase is as important as the contraction.
Repeat 10 times. Perform 3 sets daily.
formCues: ✅ Lift and squeeze gently — not a max effort contraction
✅ Fully relax between reps — incomplete relaxation leads to hypertonic PF
✅ Breathe naturally throughout — never hold breath
commonMistakes: ❌ Bearing down instead of lifting — think UP not DOWN
❌ Engaging glutes, inner thighs, or abs — isolate the PF
❌ Skipping the relaxation phase — hypertonicity is a real problem

breathing.pattern: Exhale during contraction, inhale during relaxation

injuryNotes.lower_back: PF engagement supports spinal stability. No spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Progressive pelvic floor endurance training targeting slow-twitch fibers for sustained support.
whyForYou: Your core rehabilitation starts at the pelvic floor — it's the base of the core canister that protects your back.
proTip: Do one set in each position: lying, sitting, standing. Standing is hardest — gravity works against you.
source: Pelvic Floor Physical Therapy Standards / ACOG

name: Pelvic Floor Quick Flicks

prerequisites.notes: Fast-twitch pelvic floor training. Targets the quick-response muscles that activate during coughs, sneezes, jumps.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 20 quick flicks with clear contraction-release cycle

steps: Sit or stand comfortably.
Quickly contract and fully release the pelvic floor — like a rapid on-off switch.
Each contraction is about 1 second. Full release between each.
Perform 10-20 rapid flicks per set. Rest 30 seconds between sets.
formCues: ✅ Quick, sharp contractions — not sustained holds
✅ Full release between each flick — don't hold residual tension
✅ Keep breathing natural — don't hold breath
commonMistakes: ❌ Not fully releasing between flicks — this builds tension, not power
❌ Using accessory muscles (glutes, abs) — isolate the PF
❌ Going too fast and losing the contraction quality

breathing.pattern: Breathe naturally — don't sync breath to flicks

injuryNotes.lower_back: No spinal load or movement.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Trains fast-twitch pelvic floor fibers for reactive support during coughs, sneezes, impacts, and sudden movements.
whyForYou: Fast-twitch PF fibers activate during heavy lifts — these support intra-abdominal pressure that protects your back.
proTip: Practice a quick flick right before you cough or sneeze — this is the functional application.

name: Diaphragm-Pelvic Floor Coordination Breathing

prerequisites.notes: Coordinates the diaphragm and pelvic floor — they move in opposition. Foundation for all bracing.

steps: Lie supine or sit comfortably. One hand on chest, one on belly.
INHALE through nose (4 counts): belly rises, pelvic floor RELAXES and descends naturally.
Brief pause (1 count).
EXHALE through mouth (6 counts): belly draws in, pelvic floor LIFTS and engages automatically.
formCues: ✅ Inhale = PF relaxes down. Exhale = PF lifts up. They move in sync with the diaphragm.
✅ Chest should NOT rise — all movement is in the belly
✅ The PF engagement on exhale should feel natural, not forced
commonMistakes: ❌ Reversing the pattern — holding PF tight during inhale prevents full diaphragm descent
❌ Chest-breathing instead of diaphragmatic — hand on belly should move more than hand on chest
❌ Forcing the PF instead of letting it respond to the breath
coreBracing: NATURAL — this IS the bracing mechanism. Let it happen through breathing.

breathing.pattern: Inhale: PF descends. Exhale: PF ascends. This is the core canister in action.

injuryNotes.lower_back: This is the foundation of spinal stabilization. Master this before all loaded exercises.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Coordinates the diaphragm and pelvic floor as a unit — the foundation of all core bracing and intra-abdominal pressure regulation.
whyForYou: This is THE most important exercise for your back. Your core canister (diaphragm + TVA + PF + multifidus) is what protects your surgical site.
proTip: Practice this before every single training session. It's your core's on-switch.
source: Pelvic Floor PT / Diaphragmatic Breathing Research / Core Canister Model

name: Jaw Opening with Tongue on Roof

prerequisites.notes: TMJ rehabilitation. Controlled mandibular depression with tongue stabilization prevents anterior disc displacement.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full opening without clicking or deviation

steps: Sit upright with good posture. Place the tip of your tongue on the roof of your mouth behind your front teeth.
Keeping the tongue in place, slowly open your mouth as wide as comfortable — the tongue limits how far you can open.
Hold for 1 second at maximum opening. Close slowly.
Repeat 10 times. The jaw should move straight down without deviating left or right.
formCues: ✅ Tongue stays on the roof of the mouth throughout — this is the control mechanism
✅ Jaw opens straight down — watch in a mirror for deviation
✅ Slow, controlled movement — no snapping open or closed
commonMistakes: ❌ Tongue slipping off the roof — this removes the protective mechanism
❌ Opening too wide — pain means you've gone too far
❌ Jaw deviating to one side — the movement should be symmetrical
coreBracing: NONE — completely relaxed body, focus on jaw only

injuryNotes.lower_back: No involvement. Sit comfortably.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No involvement. Relax shoulders away from ears.

purpose: Restores controlled mandibular depression. Tongue placement prevents anterior disc displacement and retrains proper jaw mechanics.
whyForYou: Jaw tension often increases during heavy exertion. This helps you maintain relaxed jaw during training — less tension = better performance.
proTip: Use a mirror to watch for jaw deviation. If it curves to one side, that's the tight side — it needs more attention.
source: TMJ Physical Therapy / DPT Clinical Guidelines

name: Lateral Jaw Slides

prerequisites.notes: TMJ lateral pterygoid mobility. Restores side-to-side jaw movement.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Equal range both sides without clicking

steps: Sit upright. Slightly open your mouth (about 1 finger-width).
Slowly slide your lower jaw to the LEFT as far as comfortable. Hold 1 second.
Return to center. Slide to the RIGHT. Hold 1 second.
Repeat 10 times each side. Movements should be equal range and smooth.
formCues: ✅ Keep the movement slow and controlled
✅ Equal range of motion both sides — the tight side needs extra attention
✅ Keep upper teeth still — only the lower jaw moves
commonMistakes: ❌ Moving the entire head instead of just the jaw
❌ Clicking or popping during the slide — reduce range until silent

injuryNotes.lower_back: No involvement.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No involvement. Relax shoulders.

purpose: Restores lateral pterygoid mobility and balanced side-to-side jaw movement. Reduces TMJ asymmetry.
whyForYou: Balanced jaw mechanics reduce tension headaches and neck tension — both of which can affect your training performance.
proTip: If one side has less range, spend 50% more time on that side. Asymmetry drives TMJ problems.
source: TMJ Physical Therapy / DPT Clinical Guidelines

name: Resisted Jaw Opening

prerequisites.notes: TMJ strengthening. Isometric resistance against jaw opening to build stabilizer strength.

steps: Sit upright. Place your fist under your chin.
Try to open your mouth while your fist resists the movement — isometric hold.
Hold 5 seconds at about 50% effort. You should feel the muscles under your chin working.
Relax completely. Repeat 10 times.
formCues: ✅ Moderate effort only — 50% of your max, not 100%
✅ The jaw should NOT actually move — this is isometric
✅ Keep the head and neck in neutral — don't push your head down
commonMistakes: ❌ Pushing too hard — this is rehabilitation, not max effort
❌ Moving the jaw against the resistance — keep it isometric
❌ Tensing the neck and shoulders — isolate the jaw muscles

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally. Exhale during the hold to stay relaxed.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No involvement.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No involvement.

purpose: Strengthens jaw stabilizers to improve joint control and reduce clicking, popping, and locking.
whyForYou: Strong jaw stabilizers mean you can keep your jaw relaxed during heavy lifts instead of clenching. Less tension, better performance.
proTip: Cue 'tongue on roof, teeth apart, lips together' during all your training — this is the ideal jaw position.
source: TMJ Physical Therapy / DPT Clinical Guidelines

name: Power Clean (from Hang)

prerequisites.notes: Elite-level Olympic lift derivative. Requires mastery of hinge pattern, front squat rack position, and barbell competency. Lower back must be severity 1 or less.

steps: Stand with barbell at hang position (mid-thigh), shoulder-width grip, hips back, chest up.
Explosively extend hips, knees, and ankles (triple extension) — shrug the bar upward.
Pull yourself UNDER the bar, catching it in a front rack position (elbows high).
Stand up from the catch position. Lower bar to hang and reset.
formCues: ✅ Triple extension is the power source — hips, knees, ankles all extend together
✅ Keep bar close to body throughout — it should brush your thighs
✅ Fast elbows in the catch — elbows whip around and UP
commonMistakes: ❌ Pulling with arms instead of driving with hips — this is a JUMP, not a curl
❌ Bar drifting away from body — causes forward pull and back strain
❌ Not getting under the bar — you pull YOURSELF down, not the bar up
coreBracing: MAXIMUM (100%) — full valsalva brace through the entire lift

breathing.pattern: Big breath and brace before pull. Hold through triple extension and catch. Exhale at the top.

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED for John (severity 3). Requires back severity 1 or less. Massive spinal loading. No exceptions.
injuryNotes.knee: Requires pain-free full squat depth for the catch. Monitor for patellar stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Requires full front rack mobility. Wrist extension critical. Labrum must be fully stable.

purpose: Maximum power development through triple extension. Develops explosive hip drive for athletic performance.
whyForYou: Currently BLOCKED due to lower back severity 3. This is a long-term goal — requires Phase 4+ AND back severity 1.
proTip: Master the hang clean pull (no catch) first. Then add the catch. Progression: pull → muscle clean → power clean.
source: NSCA Essentials of Strength Training / USAW

name: Box Jump

prerequisites.notes: Plyometric exercise. Requires knee severity 1 or less — BLOCKED for John (knee severity 2). No plyometrics with knee issues.

steps: Stand facing a box (start low — 12-16 inches). Feet hip-width, arms back.
Swing arms forward and JUMP explosively — drive hips up and forward.
Land softly on the box with BOTH feet fully on the surface. Absorb through hips and knees.
Stand up fully on the box. Step DOWN (don't jump down). Reset and repeat.
formCues: ✅ Land softly — quiet feet means good absorption
✅ Full foot on the box — heels must be ON the surface
✅ Step DOWN, don't jump down — the landing is where injuries happen
commonMistakes: ❌ Landing on toes at the edge of the box — full foot, centered
❌ Jumping DOWN off the box — always step down to protect knees
❌ Knees caving inward on landing — push knees out
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — brace on takeoff, maintain through landing

breathing.pattern: Inhale and brace before jump. Hold through flight. Exhale on landing.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep spine neutral during jump and landing. No loaded version.
injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED for John (severity 2). Requires knee severity 1. NO plyometrics with post-surgical knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Lower body power development through explosive hip and knee extension. Trains reactive strength and landing mechanics.
whyForYou: Currently BLOCKED due to left knee severity 2. Requires Phase 3+ AND knee severity 1. Long-term goal for athletic development.
proTip: Box height should challenge you, not scare you. If you have to tuck your knees to make it, the box is too high.
source: NSCA Essentials of Strength Training / Plyometric Training Guidelines

name: Medicine Ball Slam

prerequisites.notes: Requires overhead movement and explosive spinal flexion. Lower back and shoulder must be well-managed.

steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width, holding a slam ball at chest height.
Reach the ball overhead with extended arms — rise onto toes for full extension.
Slam the ball down HARD directly in front of you — drive hips back, crunch abs.
Catch the ball on the bounce (or pick it up). Reset and repeat explosively.
formCues: ✅ Use your WHOLE body — hips, core, and arms all drive the slam
✅ Slam in front of your feet, not between them — protect your face
✅ Full extension overhead before the slam — long arc = more power
commonMistakes: ❌ Using only arms — this is a full-body power movement
❌ Rounding the lower back on the slam — hinge at hips
❌ Slamming between your feet — ball can bounce into your face
coreBracing: HIGH (60-70%) — brace through the slam, especially the overhead-to-slam transition

breathing.pattern: Inhale reaching up. Forceful exhale on the slam — like a martial arts kiai.

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED for John (severity 3). Requires severity 2 or less. The overhead-to-flexion movement stresses the spine significantly.
injuryNotes.knee: Minimal knee stress — primarily hip-driven. Safe if knee severity 3 or less.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Requires full overhead ROM pain-free. BLOCKED for John (severity 2 with end-range restriction in Phase 1-2).

purpose: Full-body power development combining upper body pull, core flexion power, and stress release.
whyForYou: Currently BLOCKED due to back (sev 3) and shoulder restrictions. Future goal — battle ropes are your current power alternative.
proTip: Use a dead ball (no bounce) for maximum power expression. Slam ball if you want a catch-and-repeat rhythm.
source: NSCA Sport Performance / ACE Power Training Guidelines

name: Medicine Ball Rotational Throw

prerequisites.notes: Rotational power for combat sports (BJJ, Muay Thai). Requires anti-rotation mastery first.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 throws each side with full hip rotation, no pain

steps: Stand sideways to a wall, 3-4 feet away. Hold a medicine ball at hip height on the far side.
Load by rotating away from the wall — hips turn, back foot pivots.
Explosively rotate hips toward the wall and throw the ball against it.
Catch the rebound (or pick up). Reset. Complete all reps on one side, then switch.
formCues: ✅ Power comes from the HIPS — not the arms. Think of throwing from your back hip.
✅ Back foot pivots — heel comes up as hips rotate through
✅ Arms are the delivery system, not the engine
commonMistakes: ❌ Throwing with arms only — the hips must initiate the rotation
❌ Feet staying planted — the back foot MUST pivot
❌ Rounding the back during the throw — stay tall and rotate
coreBracing: HIGH (60%) — brace through the rotation to protect the spine

breathing.pattern: Load on inhale. Explosive exhale on throw — like a combat sport strike.

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED for John (severity 3). Rotational loading on a post-surgical spine is high-risk. Use Pallof press instead.
injuryNotes.knee: Minimal knee stress. The pivot is from the hip. Safe if knee allows standing rotation.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep ball at chest/hip level — no overhead component. Should be tolerable for moderate labrum issues.

purpose: Develops rotational power essential for combat sports (BJJ, Muay Thai), golf, and throwing activities.
whyForYou: Currently BLOCKED due to back severity 3. Future goal for your combat sports (BJJ, Muay Thai). Pallof press is your current rotational training.
proTip: Film yourself from above — you should see your belt buckle face the wall at release. If not, your hips aren't rotating enough.
source: NSCA Sport Performance / Combat Sport Conditioning

name: Sled Push

prerequisites.notes: Concentric-only exercise — no eccentric phase means minimal muscle soreness. Excellent for conditioning.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 4x40m at moderate load without form breakdown

steps: Load the sled appropriately. Grip the high handles with arms extended.
Lean into the sled at a 45-degree angle — body forms a straight line from head to ankles.
Drive one leg at a time, pushing through the forefoot. Keep driving with short, powerful steps.
Maintain the forward lean throughout. Stop at the end of the distance.
formCues: ✅ 45-degree forward lean — like sprinting against a wall
✅ Short, powerful steps — drive through the balls of your feet
✅ Arms locked out — push from the legs, not the arms
commonMistakes: ❌ Standing too upright — you lose mechanical advantage
❌ Taking long strides — short, quick steps are more powerful
❌ Holding breath — breathe rhythmically with your steps
coreBracing: HIGH (60-70%) — maintain rigid torso throughout the push

breathing.pattern: Rhythmic breathing matched to step cadence. Don't hold breath.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No eccentric spinal loading — safer than most power exercises. Keep spine neutral throughout. Use lighter loads initially.
injuryNotes.knee: Concentric-only = joint-friendly. Start light. If knee pain occurs, reduce load or switch to arm-only pushing.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep arms locked out, not actively pressing. Minimal shoulder demand.

purpose: Full-body conditioning with zero eccentric load — less soreness, faster recovery. Excellent GPP (general physical preparedness).
whyForYou: One of the safest power exercises for your back — no eccentric spinal loading. Great conditioning tool once you reach Phase 3.
proTip: Heavy sled + slow speed = strength. Light sled + fast speed = conditioning. Vary both.
source: NSCA Conditioning / Westside Barbell GPP Methods

name: Battle Ropes

prerequisites.notes: High-intensity upper body cardio. Requires shoulder stability and cardiovascular base.

steps: Stand in athletic position — feet wide, knees bent, hips back. Hold one rope end in each hand.
Alternating waves: rapidly raise and lower each arm to create waves down the rope.
Keep waves powerful — the wave should reach the anchor point.
Maintain for 20-30 seconds. Rest and repeat.
formCues: ✅ Power comes from the hips and core — don't just use arms
✅ Athletic stance — knees bent, chest up, slight forward lean
✅ Waves should be big and forceful, not small and frantic
commonMistakes: ❌ Standing upright with locked knees — stay in athletic stance
❌ Only using arms — hips and core should drive the movement
❌ Ropes going slack — maintain tension throughout
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — maintain brace throughout to protect spine during high-velocity movement

breathing.pattern: Rhythmic breathing. Don't hold breath — this is cardio.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Stay in athletic stance with neutral spine. The rope motion should not come from the back.
injuryNotes.knee: Minimal knee stress — static athletic stance. Adjust width for comfort.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep waves below shoulder height. Avoid overhead slams if labrum is sensitive. Start with short intervals.

purpose: High-intensity upper body cardio and conditioning. Excellent for combat sport preparation and metabolic training.
whyForYou: Your best power/conditioning option given shoulder and back restrictions. Waves below shoulder height keep the labrum safe. No spinal loading.
proTip: Try different patterns: alternating waves, double waves, lateral waves, circles. Each targets different muscles.

name: Sled Pull (Backward or Rope)

prerequisites.notes: Concentric-only pulling exercise. Backward walking version strengthens VMO and quads in reverse.

steps: Attach a strap or rope to the sled. Walk backward while pulling the sled toward you.
Stay low — hips down, chest up, arms pulling hand-over-hand on the rope.
Drive backward through your heels with each step.
Alternative: face away and drag the sled backward using a hip belt.
formCues: ✅ Stay low with hips back — like a backward athletic shuffle
✅ Pull with the whole body — arms, back, and legs together
✅ Keep spine neutral — don't round forward under the pull
commonMistakes: ❌ Standing too upright — you lose pulling power and strain the back
❌ Pulling only with arms — drive backward with legs simultaneously
❌ Looking over shoulder — face the sled, watch where you're pulling
coreBracing: HIGH (60-70%) — maintain rigid torso during pulling

breathing.pattern: Rhythmic breathing matched to pulling cadence

injuryNotes.lower_back: Concentric-only = joint-friendly. Keep spine neutral. Use lighter loads if back is sensitive.
injuryNotes.knee: Backward walking is excellent for VMO strengthening. Start very light. Monitor for patellar pain.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Rope pulling engages lats and biceps. Monitor for labrum strain — reduce load if shoulder clicks.

purpose: Posterior chain conditioning with concentric-only loading. Backward sled pull is excellent for knee rehabilitation and VMO strengthening.
whyForYou: Backward sled pull is one of the best VMO exercises available — targets your left knee weak point. No eccentric = low soreness.
proTip: Light backward sled pull is used by top knee rehab PTs — it strengthens the VMO without stressing the patella.
source: NSCA Conditioning / Westside Barbell / Ben Patrick (Knees Over Toes)

name: Barbell Hip Thrust

prerequisites.notes: Requires mastery of bodyweight glute bridge. Barbell adds significant load to hip extension pattern.

steps: Sit on floor with upper back against a bench. Roll a loaded barbell over your legs to your hip crease. Use a pad.
Feet flat, shoulder-width, slightly in front of knees. Drive through heels and squeeze glutes to lift hips.
Lock out at the top — torso and thighs form a straight line. Hold 1 second.
Lower slowly under control. Don't let hips drop. Reset at the bottom.
formCues: ✅ Drive through HEELS — not toes. This maximizes glute engagement.
✅ Chin tucked at the top — don't hyperextend the neck or lower back
✅ Full lockout = squeeze glutes HARD at the top for 1 second
commonMistakes: ❌ Hyperextending the lower back at the top — stop at neutral hip extension
❌ Feet too far forward (hamstring dominant) or too close (quad dominant)
❌ Rising onto toes — heels must stay planted
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — brace to prevent lower back hyperextension at the top

breathing.pattern: Inhale at the bottom. Exhale and brace as you drive up.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Relatively back-friendly when done correctly — no spinal flexion or axial loading. But hyperextension at top is the risk. Tuck chin, brace core.
injuryNotes.knee: Minimal knee stress — primarily hip movement. If knee pain, check foot position.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Upper back rests on bench — no active shoulder involvement.

purpose: Maximum glute force production. The hip thrust loads the glute max at full hip extension where it's strongest — a unique loading profile.
whyForYou: Your back needs strong glutes to protect it. Hip thrust loads the glutes heavily without spinal load — ideal for post-surgical backs.
proTip: Place a mini band above your knees to add hip abduction cue — prevents knee caving and engages glute med.
source: Bret Contreras Hip Thrust Research / NSCA

name: Park Bench Step-Ups

prerequisites.notes: Unilateral leg strength using park bench. Bench height determines difficulty.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps each leg without holding on for balance

steps: Stand facing a park bench. Place your entire right foot on the bench surface.
Drive through your RIGHT heel to step up — don't push off the back leg.
Stand tall on the bench, squeeze the glute at the top. Tap the left foot.
Lower slowly on the RIGHT leg (3 seconds). Complete all reps, then switch legs.
formCues: ✅ Drive through the WORKING leg heel — the ground leg is just for balance
✅ Keep torso upright — don't lean forward excessively
✅ Knee tracks over toes — no valgus (knee caving)
commonMistakes: ❌ Pushing off the back leg — defeats the purpose of unilateral training
❌ Knee caving inward — push knee out over toes
❌ Dropping fast on the descent — slow 3-second lowering builds strength
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — brace to maintain upright torso on single leg

breathing.pattern: Inhale at the bottom. Exhale as you drive up.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Bodyweight-only — minimal spinal load. Keep torso upright.
injuryNotes.knee: Use a LOWER step if knee flexion is limited. Watch for left knee valgus. Stop if pain above RPE 3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement. Arms for balance only.

purpose: Unilateral leg strength and balance using available outdoor equipment. Identifies and corrects left-right imbalances.
whyForYou: Tests and builds single-leg strength — reveals imbalances between your left (injured) and right knee.
proTip: Start with a lower step (curb height) and progress to full bench height over weeks.

name: Park Bench Incline Push-Ups

prerequisites.notes: Outdoor push-up variation using park bench for incline. Adjustable difficulty by angle.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps with full ROM, no compensations

steps: Place hands on the bench seat, slightly wider than shoulder-width. Walk feet back until body is straight.
Lower chest toward the bench by bending elbows to 45 degrees from body (not flared).
Touch chest to bench (or close). Press back up until arms are fully extended.
Maintain a rigid plank position throughout — no sagging or piking.
formCues: ✅ Elbows at 45 degrees from body — not straight out to the sides
✅ Body stays in one straight line — squeeze glutes to prevent hip sag
✅ Full range of motion — all the way down, all the way up
commonMistakes: ❌ Flaring elbows to 90 degrees — causes shoulder impingement
❌ Sagging hips — brace core and squeeze glutes
❌ Short range of motion — chest should reach the bench

breathing.pattern: Inhale on the way down. Exhale pressing up.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Incline reduces core demand vs floor push-up. Squeeze glutes to protect back.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Incline reduces shoulder load significantly. Keep elbows at 45 degrees. Stop if labrum clicks.

purpose: Horizontal push pattern outdoors. Incline reduces load compared to floor — ideal for beginners or shoulder-sensitive individuals.
whyForYou: The incline reduces shoulder load — friendlier for your labrum than floor push-ups. Perfect outdoor pressing exercise.
proTip: The higher the incline, the easier. Start with the bench backrest (more upright), progress to the seat (lower).

name: Park Bench Tricep Dips

prerequisites.notes: Bench dips require shoulder extension under load. Contraindicated for anterior shoulder instability.

steps: Sit on bench edge, hands gripping the seat beside your hips, fingers forward.
Slide your hips off the bench. Legs extended or knees bent (bent = easier).
Lower your body by bending elbows to 90 degrees — keep back close to the bench.
Press back up until arms are straight. Don't lock elbows aggressively.
formCues: ✅ Keep back close to the bench — don't drift forward
✅ Elbows point straight back — don't let them flare
✅ Lower to 90 degrees ONLY — deeper puts shoulder at risk
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too deep — past 90 degrees stresses the anterior shoulder capsule
❌ Shoulders shrugging up to ears — press shoulders DOWN
❌ Pushing hips forward instead of straight up — vertical motion

injuryNotes.lower_back: Minimal spinal load in seated position. Safe for back issues.
injuryNotes.knee: Bent knees reduce load. Extended legs increase it. Choose based on comfort.
injuryNotes.shoulder: HIGH RISK for labrum. Do NOT go past 90 degrees. If any clicking, popping, or pain — STOP and substitute bench push-ups.

purpose: Tricep and anterior shoulder strength using outdoor equipment. Common outdoor calisthenics exercise.
whyForYou: Proceed with CAUTION — your left labrum may not tolerate the shoulder extension position. Use shallow ROM and monitor closely.
proTip: Bent knees = easier. Straight legs = harder. Feet elevated on another bench = hardest.

name: Hill Sprints (Short)

prerequisites.notes: High-intensity sprint. Requires knee severity 1 — BLOCKED for John (severity 2). Incline reduces impact vs flat sprints.

steps: Find a moderate hill (15-30% grade). Warm up with 5-10 minutes of walking and dynamic stretching.
Sprint uphill at 90-100% effort for 10-20 seconds.
Walk back down slowly — this IS your rest period (2-3 minutes).
Repeat 4-6 sprints. Cool down with 5 minutes easy walking.
formCues: ✅ Drive knees high and pump arms aggressively
✅ Lean INTO the hill — let the incline support your sprint posture
✅ Land on forefoot/midfoot — heels barely touch on the incline
commonMistakes: ❌ Starting too fast and burning out — build into max speed over the first few steps
❌ Running back down — WALK down. The descent is where injuries happen.
❌ Not warming up adequately — sprint pulls happen to cold muscles
coreBracing: MODERATE (40%) — maintain trunk stability during high-velocity movement

breathing.pattern: Rapid rhythmic breathing during sprint. Deep recovery breathing on walk down.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Incline reduces impact vs flat sprints. Keep core braced. Stop if any radiating pain.
injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED for John (severity 2). Requires knee severity 1. High-impact sprinting is contraindicated post-surgical knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement. Arms swing naturally.

purpose: Maximum cardiovascular and power output. Hill reduces impact vs flat sprints while increasing intensity. Builds speed, power, and VO2 max.
whyForYou: Currently BLOCKED due to left knee severity 2. Trail walking is your outdoor cardio until knee clears to severity 1.
proTip: Start with 4 sprints of 10 seconds. Add 1 sprint per week. Never add duration AND volume in the same week.

name: Trail Walking with Incline

prerequisites.notes: Zone 2 cardio on uneven terrain. Builds ankle stability and general conditioning.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 30 minutes sustained walking on moderate terrain without pain

steps: Choose a trail with moderate terrain — some incline, some flat, some uneven ground.
Walk at a conversational pace (Zone 2 — can talk but slightly breathless).
Maintain tall posture. Engage core lightly on uneven terrain for stability.
Build duration over weeks: start at 15 minutes, progress to 45+ minutes.
formCues: ✅ Walk tall — don't slouch forward even on uphills
✅ Shorten stride on downhills to reduce knee impact
✅ Land midfoot on uneven terrain — avoid heel-striking on rocks
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too fast — this is Zone 2, not a race
❌ Long strides downhill — shorter steps protect the knees
❌ Staring at your feet — look 10-15 feet ahead for better balance
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — gentle engagement for stability on uneven terrain

breathing.pattern: Nasal breathing at conversational pace. Mouth breathing is ok on steep uphills.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Walking is therapeutic for disc nutrition. Avoid steep downhills that jar the spine.
injuryNotes.knee: Shorten stride on downhills. Use trekking poles if available to reduce knee load by 20-30%.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement. Trekking poles add upper body engagement if desired.

purpose: Zone 2 cardio on natural terrain. Builds ankle stability, proprioception, and cardiovascular base while being low-impact.
whyForYou: Your best outdoor cardio option. Zone 2 improves recovery between sessions. Uneven terrain builds the ankle stability for your hiking goals.
proTip: Walking poles reduce knee load by 25% on descents — highly recommended for your left knee.
source: ACSM Cardio Guidelines / Zone 2 Training Research

name: Playground Pull-Ups / Hanging

prerequisites.notes: Requires pull-up readiness (assessed through inverted rows and dead hangs). Shoulder must tolerate overhead position.

steps: Find a sturdy bar (playground monkey bars, park fitness equipment). Grip overhand, shoulder-width.
Dead hang first — relax shoulders, decompress spine, hold 15-30 seconds.
For pull-up: retract shoulder blades DOWN first, then pull chin over the bar.
Lower slowly (3 seconds) to full dead hang. Reset and repeat.
formCues: ✅ Initiate with shoulder blade retraction — pull blades DOWN and BACK first
✅ Full range of motion — dead hang to chin over bar
✅ Slow eccentric (3 seconds down) builds more strength than fast reps
commonMistakes: ❌ Kipping or swinging — strict form builds real strength
❌ Starting from a bent-arm position — full dead hang every rep
❌ Craning neck to get chin over — your body should rise, not your chin
coreBracing: MODERATE (40%) — engage core to prevent swinging and protect lower back

breathing.pattern: Inhale at the bottom. Pull while holding. Exhale on slow descent.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Dead hang decompresses the spine — therapeutic. Don't swing or kip.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement. Legs hang freely.
injuryNotes.shoulder: HIGH RISK for labrum in full overhead position. Start with dead hangs ONLY. Progress to pull-ups only if pain-free hanging for 30s.

purpose: Upper body pulling strength, grip endurance, and spinal decompression. Dead hang alone has therapeutic benefits.
whyForYou: Start with dead hangs for spinal decompression (great for your back). Progress to pull-ups only after shoulder tolerates hanging pain-free.
proTip: If you can't do a pull-up yet, do negative-only reps: jump to the top, lower yourself as slowly as possible (5-10 seconds).

name: Pendulum Shoulder Swing (Codman's)

prerequisites.notes: Post-surgical shoulder gold standard. Gravity provides gentle traction. Safe at ANY shoulder severity — this is a day-1 exercise.

steps: Bend at the hips and support yourself with your non-affected hand on a table or chair.
Let the affected arm hang straight down like a pendulum — completely relaxed.
Shift your body weight to create gentle swinging: forward/back, side-to-side, then small circles.
Perform 10 swings each direction. The arm should be PASSIVE — let gravity and momentum work.
formCues: ✅ Arm is completely relaxed — like a wet noodle hanging from your shoulder
✅ Movement comes from shifting your BODY, not from the arm muscles
✅ Start with small arcs and gradually increase
commonMistakes: ❌ Actively swinging the arm with shoulder muscles — it must be PASSIVE
❌ Tensing the arm or gripping — let it hang completely loose
❌ Going too big too fast — start small, increase gradually
coreBracing: MINIMAL (10%) — just enough to support the bent-over position

breathing.pattern: Slow, relaxed breathing. The exercise should feel calming, not effortful.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Lean on a surface for support. Don't stay bent over if back is uncomfortable — try seated version.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement. Can be done seated.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Safe at ALL severity levels. This is often the first exercise prescribed post-surgery. If painful, reduce arc size.

purpose: Gentle passive shoulder ROM using gravity. Creates joint space through gentle traction. First-line exercise for frozen shoulder and post-op shoulder.
whyForYou: Excellent daily maintenance for your left labrum. Decompresses the joint and maintains ROM without any active muscle engagement.
proTip: Hold a light weight (1-2 lbs) to increase the traction effect. The weight should pull the arm, not be lifted.
source: Codman's Pendulum Exercise / Shoulder Rehabilitation Protocols

name: Scapular Clock Exercise

prerequisites.notes: Scapular motor control exercise. Targets dyskinesis — poor scapular movement patterns.

steps: Face a wall. Place your hand at 12 o'clock position (overhead) with light pressure against wall.
Hold for 5-10 seconds while maintaining proper scapular position (no winging).
Move hand to 3 o'clock (arm out to side), then 6 o'clock (down), then 9 o'clock (across body).
At each position, hold isometrically while focusing on scapular stability. 5 reps each.
formCues: ✅ Scapula stays flat against ribcage at every position — no winging
✅ Light pressure against wall — just enough to activate stabilizers
✅ Shoulder blade moves WITH the arm, not independently
commonMistakes: ❌ Scapula winging (lifting off ribcage) — serratus anterior is weak
❌ Shrugging at overhead position — depress shoulder blade
❌ Pressing too hard into wall — light pressure only

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally through each hold. Don't hold breath.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Standing exercise. No spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Skip the 12 o'clock position if overhead is painful. Start at 3 and 9 o'clock only.

purpose: Retrains scapular motor control — the scapula must move correctly for the shoulder to function safely. Addresses dyskinesis.
whyForYou: Your left labrum depends on perfect scapular control. A winging or poorly tracking scapula increases labrum stress dramatically.
proTip: Have someone watch your scapula from behind. If it wings or tilts, you've found your weak link.
source: Scapular Dyskinesis Rehabilitation / DPT Clinical Guidelines

name: Chin Tuck with Band Resistance
primaryMuscles: Deep Cervical Flexors (Longus Colli, Longus Capitis)

prerequisites.notes: Progression from bodyweight chin tuck. Band adds resistance to strengthen deep cervical flexors.

steps: Sit upright. Loop a light resistance band around the back of your head at ear level.
Hold the band ends in front of you at forehead height, creating gentle forward pull.
Perform a chin tuck — draw chin straight back (double chin motion) AGAINST the band resistance.
Hold 5-10 seconds. Relax. Repeat 10 times.
formCues: ✅ Chin moves straight BACK — not down. Think 'make a double chin'
✅ Eyes stay level — don't tilt your head up or down
✅ Light band tension only — this is rehab, not strength training
commonMistakes: ❌ Tucking chin DOWN toward chest — the motion is straight back, horizontal
❌ Using too much band resistance — light tension only
❌ Jutting the jaw forward instead of retracting the whole head
coreBracing: NONE — relaxed posture, focus on cervical muscles only

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally. Exhale during the hold to stay relaxed.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No involvement. Seated position.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Holding the band requires mild arm engagement. Rest elbows on a surface if shoulder is sensitive.

purpose: Strengthens deep cervical flexors with resistance — the muscles that maintain proper head position and prevent forward head posture.
whyForYou: Forward head posture increases neck and shoulder strain. Strong deep cervical flexors protect against this during desk work and training.
proTip: Use the lightest band possible. If you can't feel the deep muscles at the front of your neck, the band is too heavy.
source: Cervical Stabilization Research / DPT Clinical Guidelines

name: Cervical SNAG (Self-Mobilization with Towel)

prerequisites.notes: Self-mobilization for chronic neck stiffness. Based on Mulligan SNAG technique adapted for self-treatment.

steps: Sit upright. Wrap a towel around the back of your neck at the stiff segment.
Hold one end of the towel in each hand, angled slightly upward (toward the eyes).
Pull the towel gently forward and upward while simultaneously turning your head in the restricted direction.
The towel provides a glide of the vertebra while you move — this should feel freeing, NEVER painful.
formCues: ✅ Towel angle: pull toward the EYES, not straight forward
✅ The movement should be PAIN-FREE — if it hurts, stop immediately
✅ Combine the towel pull with the head turn simultaneously
commonMistakes: ❌ Pulling towel too hard — gentle sustained pressure only
❌ Wrong angle — towel should glide the vertebra upward and forward
❌ Continuing if painful — this technique should ONLY feel better, not worse
coreBracing: NONE — relaxed body, focused on cervical movement

breathing.pattern: Exhale during the mobilization to promote relaxation

injuryNotes.lower_back: No involvement.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Holding the towel requires minimal arm effort. Rest elbows on table if needed.

purpose: Self-mobilization technique for cervical stiffness based on Mulligan Sustained Natural Apophyseal Glides. Restores segmental motion.
whyForYou: Neck stiffness from desk work and stress can refer tension into your shoulders. Keeping cervical spine mobile protects your shoulder rehab.
proTip: If this technique provides immediate relief, it's the right technique. If it doesn't help or worsens, stop and reassess.
source: Mulligan Concept SNAG Technique / Manual Therapy Evidence Base

name: Prone Press-Up with Exhale (McKenzie Variant)

prerequisites.notes: McKenzie press-up variant specifically for post-fusion adjacent segment management. Exhale cue prevents valsalva.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps with full exhale, no pain, centralization occurring

steps: Lie face-down (prone) with hands placed under shoulders, as for a push-up.
EXHALE as you press your upper body up, keeping hips on the floor.
Rise only as far as comfortable — the exhale prevents dangerous pressure buildup.
Hold 1 second at the top. Lower slowly on the inhale. Repeat 10 times.
formCues: ✅ EXHALE on the way UP — this is the critical safety modification
✅ Hips stay glued to the floor — this is spinal extension, not a push-up
✅ Let the lower back sag and relax — don't clench the glutes
commonMistakes: ❌ Holding breath during the press-up — exhale prevents dangerous intra-abdominal pressure
❌ Lifting hips off the floor — only the upper body rises
❌ Pushing through pain — centralization (pain moving toward midline) is OK, peripheralization is NOT
coreBracing: MINIMAL — let the back relax and extend passively. Exhale provides natural gentle brace.

breathing.pattern: EXHALE going UP (critical for post-fusion). Inhale lowering down.

injuryNotes.lower_back: The exhale modification makes this safer for post-fusion adjacent segments. No valsalva = less disc pressure.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Pressing up requires shoulder extension. Use elbows (prone on elbows version) if shoulder is painful.

purpose: McKenzie extension for post-fusion adjacent segment management. The exhale cue prevents dangerous pressure buildup at fusion levels.
whyForYou: Your post-surgical back benefits from daily extension work. The exhale cue is critical — it reduces disc pressure during the movement.
proTip: Audibly exhale — the sound ensures you're actually releasing air, not just pretending to exhale.

name: Hooklying March (Post-Fusion Safe)

prerequisites.notes: Post-fusion safe core activation. Zero spinal motion — all movement comes from the hips. Safe at any back severity.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 20 reps (10 each side) with lower back stable on floor

steps: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat on floor (hooklying position). Press lower back into floor.
Brace core gently (30%). Slowly lift one foot off the floor — knee stays bent at 90 degrees.
Lift until thigh is vertical (hip at 90 degrees). Hold 1 second.
Lower slowly to floor. Repeat with opposite leg. Alternate for 10 reps each side.
formCues: ✅ Lower back stays PRESSED INTO THE FLOOR throughout — this is the #1 priority
✅ Knee stays bent at 90 degrees — don't straighten the leg
✅ Move SLOWLY — if you speed up, you lose core control
commonMistakes: ❌ Lower back arching off floor — reduce range of motion until you can control it
❌ Holding breath — breathe normally, exhale on the lift
❌ Using momentum — slow, controlled marching is the goal
coreBracing: MODERATE (30%) — press lower back into floor and maintain throughout

breathing.pattern: Exhale as you lift the leg. Inhale as you lower. Back stays pressed into floor.

injuryNotes.lower_back: THE safest core exercise for post-surgical backs. Zero spinal motion. All hip movement. Start here.
injuryNotes.knee: Hip flexion only — knee angle stays constant. No knee stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms rest at sides or on belly. No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Core activation without ANY spinal motion. Post-fusion safe because the spine stays completely still while the hips move.
whyForYou: Your #1 core exercise. Zero spinal motion means zero risk to your surgical site. This is where your core training starts every session.
proTip: Place your hands on your hip bones to feel them stay level. If one side tilts, your core isn't stabilizing enough.

name: Pelvic Clock Exercise (Supine)

prerequisites.notes: Lumbopelvic dissociation exercise. Teaches independent pelvic motion — critical for post-fusion and core control.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full clock circles in both directions with smooth control

steps: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat. Imagine a clock face under your pelvis (12 = belly button, 6 = tailbone).
Tilt your pelvis to 12 o'clock (flatten lower back into floor) — then to 6 o'clock (arch lower back).
Move to 3 o'clock (tilt right hip toward floor) — then 9 o'clock (tilt left).
Combine into a smooth circle: 12 → 3 → 6 → 9 → 12. Then reverse direction.
formCues: ✅ Only the pelvis moves — upper back and feet stay still
✅ Small, controlled movements — this is about awareness, not range
✅ Smooth transitions between 'hours' — no jumping between positions
commonMistakes: ❌ Moving the upper back or legs — isolate the pelvis only
❌ Making the circles too big — subtlety is the goal
❌ Rushing — slow and deliberate builds motor control
coreBracing: MINIMAL (10-15%) — gentle engagement, focus on pelvic awareness

breathing.pattern: Breathe naturally. Don't sync breathing to movement — just relax.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Extremely gentle lumbopelvic motion. Safe for post-fusion as the motion is minimal and self-controlled.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Teaches lumbopelvic dissociation — the ability to move the pelvis independently from the spine. Foundation for all core and hip exercises.
whyForYou: After back surgery, pelvic awareness is often lost. This exercise retrains your brain-pelvis connection for better core control.
proTip: Do this exercise FIRST before any core work — it's your nervous system warm-up for the pelvis.
source: Feldenkrais Method / Post-Fusion Rehabilitation / Motor Control Research

name: Tall Kneeling Balance

prerequisites.notes: Hip/core integration without spinal load. Kneeling removes ankle and foot variables.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 30s hold with eyes closed without losing balance

steps: Kneel on a padded surface, knees hip-width apart, tops of feet flat on floor.
Stack your body: ankles → knees → hips → shoulders → ears in one vertical line.
Squeeze glutes hard. Brace core. Don't let your lower back arch.
Hold 15-30 seconds. Progress by closing eyes or adding arm movements.
formCues: ✅ Squeeze glutes HARD — they prevent lower back hyperextension
✅ Straight vertical line from knee to ear — no leaning
✅ Ribs down, core braced — don't flare the ribcage
commonMistakes: ❌ Arching the lower back — tuck pelvis slightly and squeeze glutes
❌ Leaning forward from the hips — stay completely vertical
❌ Relaxing the core — maintain active brace throughout
coreBracing: MODERATE (40%) — active brace to maintain neutral spine while kneeling

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally through the hold. Maintain brace while breathing.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No spinal load — body weight is supported through hips and knees. Squeeze glutes to protect back.
injuryNotes.knee: Pad knees well. If kneeling is painful, use hooklying march instead.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement unless adding arm challenges.

purpose: Hip and core integration in a gravity-loaded position without spinal compression. Removes ankle/foot variables for pure hip-core control.
whyForYou: Trains your glutes and core to work together — the exact pattern that protects your lower back during standing exercises.
proTip: Once 30s is easy, close your eyes. Once that's easy, hold a weight at chest height. Each progression adds challenge.
source: Core Stability Research / NASM Corrective Exercise

name: Eccentric Squat on Decline Board

prerequisites.notes: Gold standard for patellar tendinopathy (Alfredson/Purdam protocol). The decline angle isolates the patellar tendon and VMO.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3x15 pain-free on single leg with bodyweight

1.tempo: 3/0/1 (3s down, use other leg to help up)

steps: Stand on a 25-degree decline board with feet hip-width apart.
Slowly squat down over 3 seconds to approximately 60-70 degrees of knee flexion (NOT full depth).
The LOWERING phase is the exercise. Use the non-affected leg (or both) to return to standing.
Repeat 15 times. Progress to single-leg when bilateral is pain-free.
formCues: ✅ SLOW eccentric — 3 full seconds on the way down
✅ The decline board shifts load to the patellar tendon — trust the angle
✅ Keep torso upright — don't lean forward
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too deep — stop at 60-70 degrees, not full squat depth
❌ Dropping fast — the SLOW lowering is the therapeutic mechanism
❌ Avoiding mild discomfort — up to 5/10 pain during exercise is acceptable per the protocol

breathing.pattern: Inhale during the slow eccentric (lowering). Exhale returning to standing.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Upright posture, minimal spinal load. Safe for back issues.
injuryNotes.knee: This IS a knee rehabilitation exercise. Up to 5/10 pain is acceptable during the protocol. If pain exceeds 5/10, reduce depth.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Gold standard eccentric loading protocol for patellar tendinopathy. The decline angle increases patellar tendon load compared to flat squats.
whyForYou: Your left knee post-surgical rehab benefits from eccentric loading. This targets the VMO and patellar tendon specifically.
proTip: No decline board? Use a rolled-up towel under your heels or stand on a slanted surface. The angle matters.
source: Alfredson Protocol / Purdam Decline Squat Study / BJSM Tendinopathy Guidelines

name: Spanish Squat (Band Behind Knees)

prerequisites.notes: Patellar tendinopathy alternative to decline squat. The band creates a posterior pull allowing more vertical tibia.

steps: Loop a heavy resistance band around a rack at knee height. Step into the band behind your knees.
Walk back until the band is taut. Feet shoulder-width, toes forward.
Sit back into a squat — the band pulls your knees back, keeping your shins vertical.
Lower to 60-70 degrees over 3 seconds. Hold briefly. Return to standing.
formCues: ✅ Sit BACK into the band — let it support your knee position
✅ Shins stay nearly vertical — this is the magic of the Spanish squat
✅ Upright torso throughout — don't fold forward
commonMistakes: ❌ Standing too close to the anchor — you need enough band tension to pull knees back
❌ Going too deep — same 60-70 degree limit as decline squat
❌ Letting knees drift forward past band — the band should keep tibia vertical
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — upright posture, minimal spinal demand

breathing.pattern: Inhale lowering. Brief hold at bottom. Exhale rising.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Very low spinal load due to upright posture and band support. Safe for back issues.
injuryNotes.knee: This IS a knee rehabilitation exercise. The vertical tibia reduces patellofemoral compression. Up to 5/10 pain acceptable.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Alternative to decline squat for patellar tendinopathy. The band provides a posterior force that keeps the tibia vertical, reducing patellofemoral compression.
whyForYou: Easier to set up than a decline board and arguably more comfortable. Great alternative for your left knee patellar tendon work.
proTip: Try isometric holds at various depths (30s at 30°, 30s at 60°) — isometrics have strong evidence for tendon pain reduction.
source: Tendinopathy Rehabilitation / Spanish Squat Clinical Studies

name: Serratus Anterior Punch (Supine)

prerequisites.notes: Scapular protraction strengthening. Targets the serratus anterior — the muscle that holds the scapula flat against the ribcage.

steps: Lie face-up holding a light dumbbell (or no weight) with one arm extended straight toward the ceiling.
Without bending the elbow, punch the weight toward the ceiling by protracting (rounding forward) the shoulder blade.
Hold the protracted position for 1 second — feel the serratus anterior engage below your armpit.
Slowly retract the shoulder blade back to the floor. Repeat.
formCues: ✅ The elbow stays locked — all movement comes from the shoulder blade
✅ Think 'punch the ceiling' — the hand should travel 2-3 inches upward
✅ Feel the muscle engage on the side of your ribcage below the armpit
commonMistakes: ❌ Bending the elbow — this becomes a tricep exercise, not serratus
❌ Using too much weight — this is rehab, 2-5 lbs is sufficient
❌ Not fully retracting between reps — the full protraction-retraction cycle matters

breathing.pattern: Exhale on the punch (protraction). Inhale on the retraction.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Supine position. No spinal load.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Start with NO weight if labrum is sensitive. The protraction movement is generally safe for labrum issues as it creates space in the joint.

purpose: Strengthens the serratus anterior to prevent scapular winging and improve overhead shoulder mechanics. Foundation for pressing safety.
whyForYou: A strong serratus anterior keeps your scapula properly positioned during pressing — critical for protecting your left labrum.
proTip: Place your opposite hand on the serratus (side ribs below armpit) to feel it contract. If you can't feel it, you're pressing too hard.
source: Scapular Stabilization Research / Shoulder Rehabilitation Protocols

name: Thoracic Extension over Foam Roller

prerequisites.notes: Thoracic kyphosis correction. Opens the mid-back. Safe for almost all populations.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full comfortable extension over roller at 3 segments

steps: Lie face-up with a foam roller positioned horizontally under your mid-back (just below the shoulder blades).
Cross arms over chest or hands behind head to move shoulder blades out of the way.
Slowly extend backward over the roller — let gravity open your thoracic spine. Hold 2-3 seconds.
Move the roller up one segment (toward head) and repeat. Work through 3-4 thoracic segments.
formCues: ✅ Only extend at the THORACIC spine — not the lower back
✅ Keep hips on the floor — don't bridge up
✅ Breathe deeply into the stretch — exhale to deepen the extension
commonMistakes: ❌ Extending at the lower back instead of mid-back — keep abs engaged to lock lumbar spine
❌ Rolling too fast along the spine — pause and extend at each segment
❌ Placing the roller on the lower back — ONLY upper/mid back
coreBracing: LIGHT (20%) — engage abs to prevent lumbar extension. The extension should happen at the thoracic spine ONLY.

breathing.pattern: Inhale to prepare. Exhale and relax into the extension. Deep breathing opens the ribcage.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep the roller at thoracic spine ONLY — never place on lumbar spine. Engage abs to prevent lumbar extension.
injuryNotes.knee: No knee involvement. Bend knees with feet flat for comfort.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hands behind head position may be uncomfortable — cross arms over chest instead.

purpose: Opens the thoracic spine and reverses kyphosis. A stiff thoracic spine forces compensations at the lumbar spine and shoulders.
whyForYou: Thoracic mobility is CRITICAL for your back and shoulder. A stiff mid-back forces your lower back and shoulder to overwork — exactly what you need to avoid.
proTip: Do this before EVERY session. 2 minutes of thoracic extension prevents hours of compensatory movement.
source: NASM Corrective Exercise / Thoracic Mobility Research

name: Standing Hip Flexor March (Band)

prerequisites.notes: Standing hip flexor strengthening with band resistance. Targets hip flexors without impingement risk.

steps: Stand on one leg. Loop a band around the foot of the working leg and anchor it low behind you.
Brace core. Lift the working knee up toward chest against the band resistance.
Raise knee to 90 degrees hip flexion. Hold 1 second at the top.
Lower slowly against the band tension. Complete all reps, then switch legs.
formCues: ✅ Stand tall — don't lean back to compensate for the band pull
✅ Control the lowering phase — don't let the band snap the leg down
✅ Stance leg glute should be active — squeeze to stay balanced
commonMistakes: ❌ Leaning backward to lift the knee higher — stay upright
❌ Using momentum instead of controlled hip flexion
❌ Neglecting the stance leg — it's working too
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — brace to prevent lumbar extension during hip flexion

breathing.pattern: Exhale as you lift the knee. Inhale lowering.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Brace core to prevent lumbar extension. If back arches, regress to hooklying march.
injuryNotes.knee: Monitor for pinching at the front of the hip. Reduce range if impingement occurs.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement. Hold a wall or chair for balance support.

purpose: Strengthens hip flexors with resistance for improved gait, stair climbing, and athletic performance. Targets iliopsoas without impingement.
whyForYou: Strong hip flexors support your walking, stair climbing, and sport activities. The band provides progressive loading without spinal stress.
proTip: Focus on the stance leg just as much — single-leg balance + hip flexor strength is a two-for-one exercise.
source: Hip Flexor Rehabilitation / Functional Movement Training

name: Pendulum Swings (Codman's)
steps: Lean forward, supporting yourself with non-affected arm on table.
Let affected arm hang completely relaxed.
Gently swing arm in small circles — clockwise 10x, counterclockwise 10x.
Gravity does the work — NO muscle effort.
formCues: ✅ Movement from body sway, not shoulder muscles
✅ Small circles — increase size as tolerated

injuryNotes.lower_back: Lean on table for support.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Safest shoulder exercise — zero muscle effort. Post-op safe.

purpose: Gravity-assisted shoulder ROM — zero muscle effort.
whyForYou: Starting point for shoulder mobility without stressing the labrum.
proTip: Use a small weight (1-2 lb) to increase traction if tolerated.

name: Passive Flexion (Wall Walk)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Can walk fingers to overhead without pain

steps: Face wall. Place fingertips on wall at waist height.
Walk fingers UP the wall slowly — go as high as tolerated.
Hold at top for 5s. Walk fingers back down.
Track your max height — it should improve over days.
formCues: ✅ Walk fingers — don't swing arm up
✅ Only go to mild stretch — no pain

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Progressive flexion ROM. Stop at first sign of pinching.

purpose: Wall-assisted shoulder flexion ROM progression.
whyForYou: Builds overhead range gradually for your labrum.
proTip: Mark your highest point on the wall with tape — visual progress tracker.

name: Passive Abduction (Side Raise ROM)
steps: Stand or sit. Use unaffected arm to assist affected arm out to the side.
Slowly raise to comfortable limit. Hold 5s.
Lower slowly with assistance.
Track your max angle.
formCues: ✅ Thumb up position protects subacromial space

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Assisted abduction. Stop at any impingement signs.

purpose: Progressive abduction ROM with assistance.
whyForYou: Builds lateral shoulder range safely.
proTip: Slide arm up a wall for guided abduction path.

name: Active-Assisted Flexion (Towel/Pulley)
steps: Hold towel over a door-mounted pulley (or use other arm).
Pull with unaffected arm to lift affected arm overhead.
Hold at top 5s. Lower controlled.
Progress to less assistance over time.
formCues: ✅ Unaffected arm does most of the work
commonMistakes: ❌ Forcing with affected arm too early

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Active-assisted — transitioning from passive to active. Labrum must tolerate overhead.

purpose: Bridge between passive and active overhead ROM.
whyForYou: Trains your shoulder to accept overhead load gradually.
proTip: A broomstick works as well as a towel for this exercise.

name: External Rotation Stretch
steps: Stand in doorway. Elbow at 90°, forearm on frame.
Rotate body AWAY from arm — stretching external rotators.
Hold 30s. Breathe into stretch.
Switch sides.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: External rotation stretch. Stop if anterior pain.

purpose: Restore external rotation ROM — critical for overhead activities.
whyForYou: Your labrum needs full ER ROM for safe pressing.
proTip: Doorframe gives consistent stretch angle.

name: Internal Rotation Stretch (Behind Back)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Can reach behind back to mid-back

steps: Hold towel behind back — one hand high, one low.
Gently pull up with top hand to stretch bottom shoulder into IR.
Hold 30s. Switch hand positions.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Internal rotation behind back. May be limited by labrum — go to comfort only.

purpose: Restore internal rotation ROM for functional activities.
whyForYou: IR is often the most limited ROM with labrum tears.
proTip: Track how far up your back you can reach — measure weekly.

name: Isometric Strengthening Progression
steps: Stand next to wall. Push arm into wall in each direction: forward, backward, outward, inward.
Hold 5s at 50% effort. No visible movement.
Progress effort from 30% → 50% → 70% over weeks.
All 4 directions, both arms.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Isometric = zero motion. Safest strengthening. Progress effort gradually.

purpose: Build rotator cuff strength without joint motion.
whyForYou: Strengthens the muscles protecting your labrum without stressing it.
proTip: Think of each direction as a separate exercise. Master one before adding load.

name: Active ROM in Pain-Free Range
steps: Standing. Move affected arm through ALL directions under own power.
Flexion (forward), abduction (side), ER/IR, horizontal adduction.
Only within PAIN-FREE range.
Gradually increase range as tolerated.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Final McKenzie shoulder step. Full active ROM = goal achieved.

purpose: Full active shoulder ROM — final step in McKenzie shoulder protocol.
whyForYou: When you can do this pain-free, your shoulder mobility is restored.
proTip: Film yourself from the front and side — compare ROM between sides.

name: Seated Knee Extension (Passive)
steps: Sit on chair. Place heel on opposite chair/surface.
Let gravity straighten the knee — no pushing.
Hold 2 min. Breathe and relax.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Seated — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Restore full knee extension passively.
whyForYou: Full extension is critical for your left knee recovery.
proTip: Place a small towel roll under the ankle (not knee) for best position.

name: Prone Knee Flexion (Heel to Buttock)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Heel reaches within 6 inches of buttock

steps: Lie face down. Bend affected knee — bring heel toward buttock.
Use a strap around ankle to assist if needed.
Hold 30s at comfortable limit. Release.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Prone — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Restore knee flexion ROM progressively.
whyForYou: Your left knee needs flexion ROM for squatting and daily activities.
proTip: Measure the gap between heel and buttock — track weekly improvement.

name: Standing Knee Extension (Step-Up Based)
steps: Stand on low step with affected leg.
Slowly straighten the knee — lock out fully.
Control the descent.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Low load.
injuryNotes.knee: Weight-bearing extension. Progress from low step.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Weight-bearing knee extension progression.
whyForYou: Bridges passive ROM to functional strength.
proTip: Start with a 2-inch step. Increase height weekly.

name: Terminal Knee Extension with Band
steps: Band behind knee. Face anchor point.
Start with slight knee bend (~20°).
Extend fully against band. Squeeze VMO.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Standing — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: VMO-specific strengthening in the most critical range.
whyForYou: VMO is your knee's #1 protector. This targets it directly.
proTip: Place hand on VMO — feel it pop at full extension.

name: Knee Flexion Loading

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full ROM flexion under body weight

steps: Wall sit at increasing depth over weeks.
Start at 45° — progress to 90° as tolerated.
Hold 20s. Track depth weekly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Wall supported.
injuryNotes.knee: Progressive flexion loading. Respect pain limits.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Progressive weight-bearing knee flexion.
whyForYou: Builds toward squat depth with your left knee.
proTip: Track angle with phone camera — measure weekly.

name: Squat Progression (Partial → Full)
steps: Start with partial squat to comfortable depth.
